consistent-hashing-theory-matches-produc.text: Consistent hashing has sublinear scaling properties (O(log N) lookup, O(K/N) migration), and production distributed systems (Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai) have adopted consistent hashing with virtual nodes as their standard for shard distribution, suggesting these theoretical properties translate to practical scalability benefits.

justifications.label: Theory-to-practice alignment — sublinear costs proven in both analysis and production

consistent-hashing-users-dynamo-cassandr.text: Real-world systems using consistent hashing include Amazon Dynamo, Apache Cassandra, Akamai CDN, Discord, Riak, Couchbase, OpenStack Swift, GlusterFS, and MinIO.

consistent-hashing-virtual-nodes-even-di.text: Virtual nodes solve uneven key distribution in consistent hashing by mapping each physical server to multiple positions on the ring, weighted by capacity.

consistent-hashing-virtual-nodes-product.text: Consistent hashing with virtual nodes is the deployed standard for shard distribution in production distributed systems, validated by Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai, and Discord.

justifications.label: Virtual nodes solve the uneven distribution problem that makes raw consistent hashing impractical; every major deployment uses them

consistent-heuristic-implies-admissible.text: A consistent (monotone) heuristic satisfies h(x) ≤ d(x,y) + h(y) for every edge; consistency implies admissibility but not vice versa

constrained-shortest-path-np-complete.text: Adding constraints to the shortest path problem (e.g., resource constraints, required intermediate vertices) makes it NP-complete.

convergent-id-design-co-optimized-with-a.text: The convergent evolution of timestamp-prefixed IDs (Snowflake/UUID v7 arriving at the same MSB-timestamp principle independently) is explained by the append-only pipeline's dominance — since LSM trees became the universal write-optimized storage engine, ID generation naturally converged on monotonically increasing timestamps that maximally exploit the append-only ingestion path, making convergent ID design a downstream consequence of append-only's universality.

justifications.label: ID design convergence is explained by the universal write-optimized pipeline that consumes the IDs

convergent-id-design-validates-timestamp.text: The independent convergence of Snowflake and UUID v7 on timestamp-prefixed sortable IDs — arriving at the same MSB-timestamp principle from different size classes (64-bit vs 128-bit) — combined with their co-optimization with LSM storage engines validates timestamp ordering as a universal distributed systems primitive; independent design lineages converge because monotonic temporal ordering simultaneously optimizes both generation (coordination-free ID minting) and storage (append-friendly sequential writes).

justifications.label: Independent convergence on timestamp-prefix IDs confirms temporal ordering as universal primitive

copy-on-write-defers-copying-until-mutat.text: Copy-on-write (COW) defers actual copying until mutation occurs, combining memory efficiency of sharing with flexibility of mutability

cost-plus-pricing-used-by-74-percent-us-.text: Approximately 74% of US companies use cost-plus pricing (production cost + predetermined profit margin), making it the most widely adopted pricing method

count-min-conservative-update-breaks-lin.text: Conservative update in count-min sketch reduces overcounting by setting each cell to max(current, estimate + c), but this optimization breaks the linearity property.

count-min-count-sketch-complementary-est.text: Count-Min Sketch and Count Sketch provide complementary frequency estimation tradeoffs: Count-Min never undercounts (biased, min-aggregation) while Count Sketch is unbiased (median-aggregation) but can undercount — the choice depends on whether overestimation or bias is more acceptable.

justifications.label: Same problem, dual error profiles — system designers choose based on whether one-sided error or unbiasedness matters more for their workload

count-min-sketch-linear-mergeable.text: Count-min sketches are linear and mergeable: adding two sketches element-wise equals sketching the union of their streams, making them suitable for distributed systems.

count-min-sketch-never-undercounts.text: The count-min sketch has one-sided error: it can only overestimate, never underestimate the true frequency count.

count-min-sketch-parameters-w-d.text: Count-min sketch parameters: w = ceil(e/epsilon) columns controls additive error magnitude, d = ceil(ln(1/delta)) rows controls failure probability.

count-min-sketch-query-returns-minimum.text: Count-min sketch point query returns the minimum value across all d rows at the hashed positions: min_j count[j, h_j(i)].

count-min-supports-distributed-merging.text: Count-Min Sketch supports distributed aggregation via linear merging while maintaining its one-sided (never-undercount) error guarantee.

justifications.label: Conservative update improves accuracy for single-node sketches but breaks the linearity property required for distributed merge — the two optimizations are mutually exclusive

count-sketch-error-scales-m2-over-sqrt-w.text: Count Sketch error bound scales with m_2/sqrt(w), where m_2 is the L2 norm of the frequency vector and w is the number of buckets per row.

count-sketch-linear-map-nonlinear-recons.text: Count Sketch is a linear map (matrix multiplication) with a non-linear reconstruction step (median).

count-sketch-pairwise-independent-hash.text: Count Sketch requires pairwise independent hash functions — both the bucket hash h_i (mapping to {1..w}) and the sign hash s_i (mapping to {+1, -1}).

count-sketch-unbiased-estimation-via-lin.text: Count Sketch achieves unbiased frequency estimation through a mathematically principled pipeline: pairwise-independent hashing with random sign flips produces a linear projection that cancels bias in expectation, followed by non-linear median reconstruction across independent rows for robustness against heavy-hitter collisions.

justifications.label: The three properties constitute a single estimation pipeline where each stage addresses a specific statistical requirement — pairwise independence ensures unbiasedness, sign flips cancel cross-talk, and median aggregation provides concentration

count-sketch-uses-median-aggregation.text: Count Sketch uses median aggregation (not mean) across d independent sketch rows to provide robustness against heavy-hitter hash collisions.

count-sketch-vs-count-min-tradeoff.text: Count Sketch uses median estimation and is unbiased but can underestimate; Count-Min Sketch uses min estimation, is biased, never underestimates, and uses less memory but has weaker error guarantees.

cpp20-added-modules-replacing-headers.text: C++ formally added module support in C++20; prior to that, header files and separate compilation served as a workaround for modular organization

crawl-delay-nonstandard-varies-by-engine.text: Crawl-delay is a nonstandard robots.txt directive: Yandex treats it as seconds between visits, Bing treats it as a time window, Google ignores it entirely

crawler-four-policies.text: Web crawlers are governed by four core policies: selection (what to download), re-visit (when to re-check), politeness (how to avoid overloading sites), and parallelization (how to coordinate distributed crawlers)

cristians-algorithm-round-trip-time-serv.text: Cristian's algorithm synchronizes clocks by querying a time server with an external time source and adjusting for network delay using round-trip time estimation

cross-cutting-concerns-require-aop.text: Cross-cutting concerns like security and logging cut across primary business logic and require special techniques such as aspect-oriented programming (AOP) to address properly

crs-vs-gds-distinction.text: A CRS (Computer Reservation System) is an airline's own reservation system; a GDS (Global Distribution System) aggregates multiple airlines and travel products into a single platform for travel agents and consumers.

crypto-hash-collision-resistance-n-over-.text: Collision resistance of an n-bit hash has security strength of n/2 bits due to the birthday paradox

crypto-hash-fixed-length-output.text: A cryptographic hash function produces a fixed-length output (digest) from arbitrary-length input

crypto-hash-preimage-resistance-n-bits.text: Pre-image resistance of an n-bit cryptographic hash has security strength of n bits

cvs-lacks-atomic-commits.text: CVS lacks atomic commits, meaning the repository can be left in an inconsistent state if an operation is interrupted — a key limitation distinguishing it from Subversion and Git

dafsa-more-space-efficient-than-trie-for.text: A DAFSA (deterministic acyclic finite state automaton) is more space-efficient than a trie when only dictionary membership is needed, because it also compresses common suffixes.

dag-condensation-contracts-sccs.text: The condensation of any directed graph — formed by contracting each strongly connected component into a single supervertex — always produces a DAG.

dag-iff-topological-ordering.text: A directed graph is a DAG if and only if it has a topological ordering — this is the definitional equivalence.

dag-shortest-longest-path-linear.text: Both shortest and longest paths in a DAG can be found in O(V+E) by processing vertices in topological order, whereas longest path in a general graph is NP-hard.

dag-shortest-path-linear-time-via-topo-s.text: Shortest paths on directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) can be found in O(E + V) time via topological sorting, regardless of whether edges have negative weights.

dag-transitive-reduction-unique.text: The transitive reduction of a DAG is unique; for cyclic directed graphs, the transitive reduction is not unique.

dag-unique-topo-order-iff-hamiltonian-pa.text: A DAG has a unique topological ordering if and only if it contains a directed Hamiltonian path (a directed path through all vertices).

data-structure-choice-more-important-tha.text: Rob Pike's rule: the choice of data structure almost always has a greater impact on efficiency than the choice of algorithm

data-structure-vs-adt-distinction.text: A data structure is a concrete physical implementation of how data is stored and accessed; an abstract data type (ADT) describes the logical interface and allowed operations without specifying implementation

dbc-coined-by-meyer-1986-eiffel.text: Design by Contract was coined by Bertrand Meyer in 1986 in connection with the Eiffel programming language

dbc-contract-checks-disabled-in-producti.text: Contract assertions are typically disabled in production/release builds for performance, resulting in zero runtime cost (e.g., C/C++ assert compiled away, Python -O flag suppresses assertions)

dbc-contracts-equivalent-to-hoare-triple.text: Design by Contract specifications (precondition, operation, postcondition) are semantically equivalent to Hoare triples: {P} S {Q}

dbc-offensive-vs-defensive-programming.text: Design by Contract uses offensive programming (fail hard on contract violations) as opposed to defensive programming where the supplier validates preconditions and handles violations gracefully

dbc-precondition-client-obligation.text: In Design by Contract, preconditions are the client's obligation (what must be true before a method is called) and postconditions are the supplier's obligation (what the method guarantees after execution)

dbc-subclass-weaken-preconditions-streng.text: In Design by Contract, subclasses may weaken preconditions but not strengthen them, and may strengthen postconditions and invariants but not weaken them, approximating the Liskov Substitution Principle

dds-canonical-brokerless-pubsub.text: DDS (Data Distribution Service) is the canonical brokerless pub/sub implementation, using IP multicast for direct publisher-subscriber discovery

deadlock-cs-definition.text: In computer science, a deadlock is a situation where two or more processes are each waiting for the other to release a resource, resulting in indefinite blocking with no process making progress

deadlock-disambiguation-lacks-technical-.text: The Wikipedia Deadlock disambiguation page contains no technical detail on CS deadlocks — the Coffman conditions, detection, prevention, and avoidance strategies are on the separate Deadlock (computer science) page

deadlock-four-coffman-conditions-necessa.text: Deadlock requires four Coffman conditions to be simultaneously satisfied: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait

deadlock-impossible-in-lock-free-systems.text: Lock-free systems (CAS-based, immutable data structures) are structurally immune to deadlock because they eliminate the mutual exclusion Coffman condition — without locks, the four necessary conditions for deadlock cannot be simultaneously satisfied.

justifications.label: Deadlock requires mutual exclusion; lock-free operations and immutable data eliminate this condition entirely

deadlock-prevention-global-lock-ordering.text: Deadlock prevention via consistent global lock ordering is the most common strategy — acquiring locks in a predetermined order prevents circular wait

debit-credit-normal-balances.text: Assets and Expenses have normal debit balances (debits increase, credits decrease); Liabilities, Capital/Equity, and Revenue have normal credit balances (credits increase, debits decrease).

decimation-by-m-keeps-every-mth-sample.text: Decimation by factor M keeps only every Mth sample, dividing the sampling rate by M (equivalently multiplying the sampling interval by M)

decimation-two-step-process-filter-then-.text: Integer downsampling requires two steps in order: (1) lowpass anti-aliasing filter to remove high-frequency components, then (2) keep every Mth sample — filtering must precede downsampling to prevent aliasing

defense-in-depth-conceived-by-nsa.text: Defense in depth is an information security strategy conceived by the NSA that deploys multiple independent layers of security controls throughout an IT system

defense-in-depth-onion-model-layers.text: The defense in depth onion model layers from inside out: Data (core), People, Network Security, Host-Based Security, Application Security

defense-in-depth-three-tiers.text: Defense in depth organizes controls into three overarching tiers: Physical, Technical, and Administrative/Operational

deleted-columns-appear-empty-until-compa.text: After deletion but before compaction, deleted columns appear as empty (null) rather than absent in query results.

delta-bidding-system-2011-bumping.text: Delta introduced a bidding system in 2011 where passengers state the minimum voucher value they would accept for voluntary bumping, reducing involuntary bumping to 3 per 100,000 passengers

design-space-spans-deterministic-sorted-.text: The data structure design space for distributed systems includes at least two complementary areas — the sorted structure design space (shaped by axes of dimensionality and storage tier, producing structures like B+ trees, skip lists, and R-trees) and the probabilistic query space (covering three orthogonal query types: membership, cardinality, and frequency) — where sorted structures provide exact-access capabilities and probabilistic structures provide scale-compensated approximation with sublinear space and distributed mergeability.

justifications.label: Sorted and probabilistic structures are complementary halves of the data structure design space — exact access vs approximate aggregation — each with its own internal orthogonal axes

dht-consistent-hashing-minimal-redistrib.text: Consistent hashing assigns keys to nodes using a distance function over the keyspace (e.g., clockwise distance on a ring) so that adding or removing a node only affects keys owned by adjacent-ID nodes, minimizing redistribution.

dht-core-abstraction.text: A distributed hash table (DHT) distributes key-value pairs across participating nodes so that any node can efficiently retrieve a value by key, combining decentralization with guaranteed lookup results.

dht-exact-match-only.text: DHTs natively support only exact-match key lookup, not keyword search or range queries (unless using locality-preserving hashing which sacrifices uniform load distribution).

dht-log-n-degree-and-route-length.text: Most DHTs (Chord, Kademlia, Pastry, Tapestry) choose O(log n) routing table degree and O(log n) route length; each node maintains O(log n) neighbors and coordinates with O(log n) other nodes for membership changes.

dht-sybil-attack-primary-weakness.text: The Sybil attack (creating many fake node identities) is the primary security weakness of DHTs; defense remains an open research problem with approaches including social trust and Byzantine fault tolerance.

di-constructor-injection-preferred-form.text: Constructor injection is the most common and preferred form of dependency injection because it guarantees the client is always in a valid state after construction

di-four-injection-types.text: There are four types of dependency injection: constructor injection, method injection, setter injection, and interface injection

di-four-roles-service-client-interface-i.text: Dependency injection involves four roles: Services (provide functionality), Clients (consume services), Interfaces (contracts), and Injectors (construct and wire the object graph)

di-not-same-as-dip.text: Dependency injection (technique) is distinct from the Dependency Inversion Principle (design principle): you can inject dependencies without following DIP and vice versa, though they are usually used together

digital-wallet-server-side-vs-client-sid.text: Server-side (thin) digital wallets are created and maintained by organizations on their servers, while client-side wallets are stored on the user's device and self-maintained

digital-wallet-two-components.text: A digital wallet has two components: a software component (providing security and encryption) and an information component (a database storing user data such as addresses and payment methods)

dijkstra-array-complexity-v-squared.text: Dijkstra's algorithm with an array/linked list has time complexity Θ(|V|²), which is optimal for dense graphs.

dijkstra-binary-heap-complexity.text: Dijkstra's algorithm with a binary heap has time complexity Θ((|E|+|V|) log |V|), simplifying to Θ(|E| log |V|) for connected graphs.

dijkstra-degenerate-case-in-astar-heuris.text: Dijkstra's algorithm sits at the zero-information end of the A* heuristic spectrum — using h(x) = 0 (trivially consistent and admissible) — while any consistent heuristic h(x) > 0 can only improve or match Dijkstra's node expansion count, never worsen it.

justifications.label: A* with h=0 recovers Dijkstra; any consistent heuristic strictly reduces search space

dijkstra-early-termination-at-target.text: Dijkstra's algorithm can terminate early once the target node is extracted from the priority queue, as its distance is then final.

dijkstra-fails-negative-edges.text: Dijkstra's algorithm does not work correctly with negative edge weights because its correctness proof relies on the invariant that adding an edge can never decrease path cost.

dijkstra-fibonacci-heap-complexity.text: Dijkstra's algorithm with a Fibonacci heap achieves Θ(|E|+|V| log |V|), the asymptotically fastest known bound for arbitrary directed graphs with unbounded non-negative weights.

dijkstra-first-mutex-solution-1965.text: Edsger W. Dijkstra first formulated and solved the mutual exclusion problem in 1965, making it the first topic in the study of concurrent algorithms

dijkstra-general-runtime-formula.text: Dijkstra's general running time is Θ(|E|·T_dk + |V|·T_em), where T_dk is the decrease-key cost and T_em is the extract-min cost of the priority queue.

dijkstra-is-astar-with-zero-heuristic.text: Dijkstra's algorithm is a special case of A* where h(x) = 0 for all nodes

dijkstra-no-negative-weights.text: Dijkstra's algorithm does not work correctly with negative edge weights; Bellman-Ford must be used instead.

dijkstra-solves-sssp-nonneg-weights.text: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths from a single source to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights.

dimensional-complexity-determines-tree-f.text: Balanced tree fill factor requirements are determined by the dimensional complexity of indexed data — one-dimensional keys (B+ trees) sustain 50% minimum fill with sibling redistribution, while multi-dimensional spatial data (R-trees) forces acceptance of 30-40% fill because overlap in higher dimensions makes tight packing and sibling rebalancing intractable.

justifications.label: Both structures share balanced-tree heritage (all leaves at same depth) but the dimensional complexity of keys dictates how aggressively space can be reclaimed

dirsync-cookie-watermark-pattern.text: DirSync uses a cookie/watermark pattern: client sends empty cookie on first sync to get all objects plus an updated cookie, then passes the stored cookie on subsequent syncs to retrieve only changes since the last query

dirsync-is-ldap-extension.text: DirSync is an LDAP server extension (not a standalone protocol) used to search an Active Directory partition for changed objects, defined in IETF draft draft-armijo-ldap-dirsync

distributed-architecture-three-pillar-fr.text: Distributed system architecture is shaped by two orthogonal universals — logarithmic cost bounds (the convergent operational floor across structures and protocols) and CAP bifurcation (the consistency-vs-availability determinant) — which define the structural design space, while immutability serves as a dual-purpose operational primitive that addresses both coordination elimination (enabling AP viability) and reliable messaging (via persistence and idempotency), making it a practical enabler within that space rather than a co-equal universal.

justifications.label: The dual universals (log bounds + CAP) constrain what's possible; immutability is the design principle that makes the AP branch and reliable event processing realizable within those constraints

distributed-cache-commodity-hardware.text: Distributed caches run on cheaper commodity web-server-tier hardware rather than expensive database-server-tier hardware.

distributed-cache-consistent-hashing-sta.text: Distributed caches in production deploy consistent hashing with virtual nodes as the standard sharding strategy — the cache-specific sharding landscape (modulus, range, consistent hashing) converges on the same endpoint as the broader distributed systems evolution.

justifications.label: Distributed cache sharding converges on consistent hashing as the production standard, matching the broader distributed systems trajectory

distributed-cache-inherits-hashing-evolu.text: Distributed caching's three main sharding strategies — modulus, range-based, and consistent hashing — parallel the dynamic hashing amortization spectrum, where each successive approach reduces the disruption cost of topology changes. Consistent hashing with virtual nodes has become the deployed standard for shard distribution in production distributed systems, as validated by its adoption in Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai, and Discord.

justifications.label: Cache sharding strategies are a local instance of the global hashing evolution — modulus = hash table resize, range = linear hashing, consistent hashing = distributed endpoint

distributed-cache-primary-use-cases.text: Distributed caches primarily store two types of data: application data from databases and web session data.

distributed-cache-three-sharding-strateg.text: The three main sharding strategies for distributed caches are modulus sharding (hash mod N), range-based sharding, and consistent hashing.

distributed-data-layer-requires-partitio.text: A complete distributed data layer must solve both the partitioning problem (where does data live?) and the ordering problem (when did events happen?), addressed respectively by consistent hashing and logical clocks — Dynamo exemplifies the combination with consistent hashing for shard distribution and vector clocks for conflict detection.

justifications.label: Partitioning and ordering are orthogonal distributed systems requirements that must both be solved

distributed-data-plane-requires-dual-pri.text: A complete distributed data plane requires two orthogonal primitives — sequential append-only persistence (for durability and write optimization) and partitioning with logical ordering (for data distribution and consistency) — and both CAP lineages must independently solve both.

justifications.label: Sequential durability (from LSM/Kafka/email convergence) and partitioning+ordering (from CAP+consistent-hashing+clocks) are independently necessary; neither subsumes the other

distributed-data-requires-persistence-di.text: A distributed data platform benefits from at least three complementary concerns: sequential append-only persistence (for durability and write optimization), partitioning with logical ordering (for data distribution and consistency), and probabilistic structures (as a primary compensation mechanism for scaling tradeoffs such as read amplification and approximate analytics) — the first two are argued as independently necessary orthogonal primitives, while probabilistic structures address practical scaling challenges that arise once those primitives operate at scale.

justifications.label: The dual primitives (persistence + partitioning) handle writes and distribution but create read amplification at scale; probabilistic compensation is the third independently necessary layer that completes the platform

distributed-data-triad-complete-when-ske.text: The complete distributed data platform — sequential persistence for durability, consistent hashing for distribution, and probabilistic structures for scale compensation — achieves full coordination-free distributed aggregation only when the probabilistic layer's algebraic mergeability is preserved, enabling sketches to be combined across partitions without centralized coordination.

justifications.label: Conservative update breaks Count-Min linearity, undermining the coordination-free merging that the compensation layer requires

distributed-db-architecture-examples.text: MongoDB, TiDB, Teradata, Greenplum, and Vertica use shared-nothing architecture; Aurora and Snowflake use shared-disk or hybrid architecture.

distributed-db-data-concerns-triad.text: The three data concerns for distributed database design decisions are security, consistency, and integrity.

distributed-db-loosely-coupled-sites.text: A distributed database consists of loosely coupled sites that share no physical components, distinguishing it from parallel database systems which are tightly coupled.

distributed-ledger-consensus-replicated.text: A distributed ledger is a consensus-based, replicated ledger shared and synchronized across multiple geographic locations, and is the foundational concept for blockchain

distributed-ordering-requires-logical-cl.text: The absence of a global clock in distributed systems makes logical ordering mechanisms (Lamport timestamps for causal ordering, vector clocks for concurrency detection) essential rather than optional for any operation requiring event sequencing.

justifications.label: No global clock is listed as a core distributed challenge; Lamport and vector clocks are the two solutions, forming a weak-to-strong spectrum

distributed-systems-governed-by-dual-uni.text: Distributed system architecture is shaped by two recurring structural forces: logarithmic cost bounds (O(log N) as a convergent operational floor observed independently across data structures and distributed protocols, though not yet demonstrated to extend from a single unified principle) and CAP bifurcation (the consistency-vs-availability choice as a total architectural determinant that cascades through the full technology stack including concurrency control). The first constrains what is efficiently achievable within each domain where it appears; the second constrains what is selectable. Together they define two prominent axes of the distributed system design space, though they may not exhaust it.

justifications.label: The two highest organizing principles are complementary axes — achievability and selectability

distributed-systems-three-core-challenge.text: The three core challenges of distributed systems are: maintaining concurrency, operating without a global clock, and handling independent component failures.

distributed-transaction-requires-coordin.text: Distributed transactions enforce ACID across multiple nodes and require a coordinating entity

distributed-vs-parallel-distinction.text: Distributed systems use private memory per node with message passing (loosely coupled); parallel systems share memory across processors (tightly coupled). The boundary is fuzzy in practice.

distribution-compensation-instantiates-t.text: The distribution-plus-compensation pattern's confirmed layer-invariance across content delivery and data ingestion layers suggests it may be a concrete architectural manifestation of how the three structural pillars interact — logarithmic bounds likely govern aspects of the distribution primitive's lookup efficiency, CAP bifurcation plausibly influences which compensation strategy is employed, and immutability supports coordination-free distribution as a practical enabler rather than a structural guarantee — though the specific causal linkages between each pillar and each layer's instantiation of the pattern remain to be fully traced.

justifications.label: Layer-invariant distribution-compensation pattern arises from all three structural forces interacting

distribution-plus-compensation-convergen.text: Web-scale infrastructure and stream processing independently converge on the same dual architecture — a distribution primitive (consistent hashing for data/request routing vs partitioning for stream parallelism) plus probabilistic compensation (sketches for approximate analytics across partition boundaries) — demonstrating that distribution + compensation is the convergent architectural pattern spanning both infrastructure and processing concerns.

justifications.label: Distribution + compensation recurs independently across infrastructure and processing domains

distribution-plus-compensation-layer-inv.text: The distribution-plus-compensation architectural pattern appears at two distinct infrastructure layers — content delivery (hierarchical caching topology with consistent hashing for request routing) and data ingestion (append-only write pipeline from timestamp IDs through LSM storage to consistent hashing distribution) — suggesting this dual-concern pattern may be layer-invariant, though the specific compensation mechanisms differ between layers (freshness management in caching vs. compaction and space optimization in storage).

justifications.label: CDN caching and write-optimized pipelines independently converge on distribution + compensation — same pattern, different layers

dlq-six-canonical-routing-reasons.text: Six canonical reasons a message routes to a DLQ: queue doesn't exist, max queue length exceeded, message exceeds size limit, TTL expired, rejected by queue exchange, and max receive/delivery attempts exceeded

dlq-vs-invalid-message-channel.text: A dead letter queue handles infrastructure/delivery failures, while an Invalid Message Channel handles application-level semantic rejection by consumers — these are distinct patterns

dlq-works-with-retry-policies.text: DLQs work in tandem with retry/redelivery policies — a message lands in the DLQ only after retries are exhausted

dotted-version-vectors-client-server-sca.text: Dotted version vectors solve the scalability problem of client-server architectures where many clients write through few servers, and are used in systems like Riak

double-entry-required-gaap-sec.text: Double-entry bookkeeping is required for GAAP, SEC, and UK Companies Act compliance; single-entry bookkeeping is insufficient for statutory financial reporting.

down-heap-swaps-with-dominant-child.text: Down-heap (sift-down) swaps with the larger child in a max-heap or smaller child in a min-heap; getting this wrong is a common exam mistake

drrip-set-dueling-srrip-brrip.text: DRRIP uses set dueling to dynamically choose between SRRIP (optimal when working set < cache) and BRRIP (optimal when working set > cache and thrashing occurs)

dual-sublinear-scaling-complete-unless-c.text: The dual sublinear scaling strategy — deterministic logarithmic bounds for lookups/routing combined with probabilistic bounds for cardinality/frequency/membership estimation — achieves complete coordination-free distributed operation only when the probabilistic sketches remain linearly mergeable, which conservative update optimization in Count-Min Sketch violates.

justifications.label: Dual sublinear scaling requires linear mergeability of the probabilistic half

dual-sublinear-scaling-deterministic-and.text: Distributed systems at scale employ two complementary sublinear scaling strategies: deterministic logarithmic bounds (O(log N) for lookups, routing, integrity proofs, and gossip dissemination) and probabilistic approximation (Bloom filters, HyperLogLog, Count-Min/Count Sketch for space-efficient membership, cardinality, and frequency estimation) — the former bounds the cost of exact operations, the latter bounds the cost of approximate operations, and together they make large-scale distributed data platforms tractable where linear-cost alternatives would be prohibitive.

justifications.label: Logarithmic bounds and probabilistic approximation are independently motivated sublinear scaling strategies that together cover the full spectrum of distributed operations — exact and approximate

dual-verification-ap-unless-integrity-at.text: The dual AP consistency mechanism — Merkle trees for structural divergence detection combined with version vectors for causal conflict resolution — provides reliable eventual consistency only when the Merkle verification layer is not compromised by second preimage attacks that could make divergent replicas appear identical.

justifications.label: Structural verification (Merkle) is the foundation for causal resolution (version vectors) — if Merkle integrity fails via second preimage attacks, divergence goes undetected and the causal layer never triggers

dual-verification-complete-ap-consistenc.text: AP eventual consistency requires two orthogonal verification mechanisms operating on different dimensions: Merkle trees detect STRUCTURAL divergence (which key ranges differ between replicas, via O(log n) hash comparison) while version vectors detect CAUSAL divergence (which writes are concurrent vs ordered, with three-outcome comparison mapping to resolve/accept/reject) — together providing both the detection of WHAT has diverged and the determination of HOW to reconcile it.

justifications.label: Merkle trees (depth-2) verify structural integrity; version vectors (depth-4) provide complete causal conflict lifecycle — the what/how orthogonality of the two verification mechanisms is emergent

durability-acid-d-committed-transactions.text: Durability (the D in ACID) guarantees that once a transaction is committed, its effects persist permanently even through crashes, power outages, and hardware failures

durability-three-failure-types.text: Database durability must handle three distinct failure types: transaction failures (application-level aborts/constraint violations), system failures (crash/power loss destroying volatile memory), and media failures (disk/SSD hardware damage)

dynamic-hashing-amortization-spectrum.text: Hash tables, linear hashing, and consistent hashing form a spectrum of increasingly sophisticated dynamic hashing — hash tables batch-resize at load factor thresholds, linear hashing grows one predetermined bucket at a time, and consistent hashing achieves sublinear O(K/N) migration via ring partitioning — each reducing the disruption cost of topology changes.

justifications.label: Three hash-based systems solve growth amortization with progressively finer granularity

dynamic-perfect-hashing-k-squared-slots.text: Dynamic perfect hashing provides guaranteed O(1) worst-case lookup by using k² slots per bucket containing k entries.

dynamic-pricing-five-methods.text: The five primary dynamic pricing methods are: cost-plus pricing, competitor-based pricing, value-based/elasticity pricing, bundle pricing, and time-based pricing

dynamo-ap-converges-with-trusted-causali.text: Dynamo's AP architecture achieves eventual consistency through vector clock-based conflict detection, providing complete causality guarantees for replica convergence.

justifications.label: Dynamo's vector-clock-based conflict resolution assumes non-Byzantine nodes — Byzantine failures undermine causality detection, breaking the convergence guarantee

dynamo-ap-doubly-validated-in-trusted-en.text: Dynamo's AP architecture achieves double validation — production deployment confirming the distribution/convergence solution AND independent theoretical/historical validation of consistent hashing as the hashing evolution endpoint — but this doubly-validated architecture remains contingent on a non-Byzantine operating environment for its causality tracking.

justifications.label: Two independent depth-4 validation chains converge on Dynamo's AP architecture, but both assume trusted participants — Byzantine failures undermine the vector clock causality tracking that the convergence mechanism depends on, regardless of how well-validated the partitioning layer is

dynamo-ap-solves-distribution-and-conver.text: Dynamo's AP architecture provides a complete distributed data solution by combining consistent hashing for partitioning (the canonical AP distribution mechanism validated at production scale) with Merkle tree anti-entropy for integrity-verified replica convergence.

justifications.label: Distribution (where data lives) and convergence (how replicas agree) are independently necessary — neither alone constitutes a complete AP data layer

dynamo-ap-system-leaderless-replication.text: Amazon Dynamo is a highly available key-value store that prioritizes availability and partition tolerance (AP in CAP theorem) using eventual consistency and leaderless replication where all nodes are symmetric peers

dynamo-blueprint-defines-ap-lineage-with.text: Dynamo's production validation not only established the AP database lineage alongside CP systems but made consistent hashing with virtual nodes the canonical data distribution mechanism for the entire AP family (Cassandra, Riak, Couchbase), creating a reusable architectural blueprint.

justifications.label: AP lineage's defining contribution was not just proving AP viability but standardizing its distribution layer

dynamo-canonicalized-hashing-evolution-e.text: Dynamo's canonicalization of consistent hashing as the AP lineage's distribution layer is the natural culmination of the hashing evolution spectrum — the amortization gradient from batch-resize (hash tables) to single-bucket (linear hashing) to zero-downtime (consistent hashing) converges exactly on the AP lineage's requirement for continuous partition management without service interruption.

justifications.label: The AP lineage's canonical distribution choice and the hashing evolution endpoint are independently derived but convergent — Dynamo selected consistent hashing because the amortization spectrum's endpoint satisfies AP's zero-downtime partitioning constraint

dynamo-convergence-integrity-verified.text: Dynamo's AP architecture achieves integrity-verified eventual consistency by combining consistent hashing for data distribution with Merkle tree anti-entropy for verifiable replica repair — but this convergence mechanism depends on Merkle tree cryptographic soundness.

justifications.label: Anti-entropy repair trusts Merkle proofs to identify divergent ranges; second preimage attacks could cause repair to accept corrupted data as valid, undermining convergence

dynamo-created-for-2004-holiday-scalabil.text: Amazon created Dynamo specifically to address scalability issues encountered during the 2004 holiday season; it was used internally in AWS services like S3 by 2007

dynamo-established-consistent-hashing-ap.text: Dynamo's production validation of the AP pattern established consistent hashing with virtual nodes as the canonical data distribution layer for availability-first distributed databases.

justifications.label: Dynamo's five core techniques and its AP design both center on consistent hashing as the partitioning foundation

dynamo-five-core-techniques.text: Dynamo's five core techniques map to specific problems: consistent hashing (partitioning), vector clocks (write availability/versioning), sloppy quorum + hinted handoff (temporary failures), Merkle trees (permanent failure recovery), and gossip protocol (membership/failure detection)

dynamo-merkle-anti-entropy-enables-ap-co.text: Dynamo's AP architecture uses Merkle trees' O(log n) integrity verification as the mechanism for detecting and repairing replica divergence, making eventual consistency practically achievable by efficiently identifying which key ranges are out of sync without full data comparison.

justifications.label: Merkle trees are the convergence mechanism that makes AP eventual consistency practical

dynamo-merkle-trees-detect-replica-diver.text: Dynamo uses Merkle trees for anti-entropy: they enable efficient comparison of replica state to detect and repair divergence between replicas after permanent failures

dynamo-paper-sosp-2007-decandia.text: The Amazon Dynamo paper was published by DeCandia et al. at SOSP 2007; Amazon never released the implementation — Cassandra, Riak, and Voldemort are open-source systems inspired by the paper

dynamo-sloppy-quorum-writes-to-available.text: Sloppy quorum differs from strict quorum by allowing writes to succeed even when some designated replicas are down, by writing to other available nodes; hinted handoff forwards missed writes to recovered replicas

dynamo-validates-ap-pattern-in-productio.text: Amazon Dynamo validated the AP architectural pattern by combining consistent hashing, vector clocks, sloppy quorums, and Merkle trees into a cohesive production system that prioritizes availability during network partitions.

justifications.label: Dynamo proved AP is viable at scale by mapping each CAP-era concern to a specific technique, spawning Cassandra and Riak

dynamo-vs-dynamodb-replication-differenc.text: DynamoDB uses single-leader replication, not leaderless replication like the original Dynamo — DynamoDB is 'built on the principles of Dynamo' but differs in this fundamental architectural choice

eafp-eliminates-toctou.text: The EAFP pattern (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission) eliminates TOCTOU by attempting the operation and handling failure rather than pre-checking state

earth-mean-radius-6371km.text: Earth's mean radius commonly used in distance calculations is approximately 6,371 km.

eda-broker-vs-mediator-topology.text: EDA has two primary topologies: broker (no central orchestrator, higher performance/scalability) and mediator (central orchestrator, better control/error handling)

eda-data-loss-prevention-persist-and-ack.text: EDA prevents data loss by persisting in-transit events and only dequeuing after the next component acknowledges receipt (client acknowledge mode and last participant support)

eda-domain-vs-integration-events.text: Domain events have lighter payloads (within a single bounded context) while integration events have heavier payloads (cross-context communication requiring more attributes)

eda-emitters-no-knowledge-of-consumers.text: In EDA, event emitters have no knowledge of consumers — this is the core decoupling property of the architecture

eda-error-handler-out-of-order.text: In EDA's async error-handler pattern, a separate processor receives failed events, attempts remediation, and resubmits to the original channel — but resubmitted events arrive out of sequence

eda-event-design-space-payload-and-proce.text: EDA's design space spans two orthogonal axes — event payload design (fat vs thin payloads trading data completeness for stamp coupling, with domain events for intra-context and integration events for cross-context boundaries) and event processing complexity (simple single-event reactions through stream processing to complex multi-event pattern correlation).

justifications.label: Three underutilized base beliefs describe orthogonal dimensions that architects must navigate simultaneously — payload shape and processing complexity are independent design choices

eda-event-is-state-change.text: In event-driven architecture, an event represents a significant change in state; what is actually produced and consumed is an asynchronous notification message about that event

eda-fat-vs-thin-payload-tradeoff.text: Fat event payloads include all data (faster but risk stamp coupling and consistency issues); thin payloads include only keys/IDs requiring consumers to fetch data (slower but lower coupling)

eda-four-processing-styles.text: EDA has four event processing styles: simple (single event reaction), event stream processing (real-time screening), complex event processing (cross-event pattern detection), and online event processing (async distributed event logs)

electricity-tou-pricing-four-models.text: Electricity dynamic pricing has four main models: time-of-use (TOU) with pre-established rates changed no more than twice yearly, critical peak pricing (TOU + wholesale on peak days), real-time pricing (changes as often as hourly), and peak-load reduction credits

electronic-market-order-priority.text: Electronic market order priority ranking: market orders > displayed limit orders (FIFO) > conditional orders > iceberg/dark orders.

email-address-format-local-at-domain.text: Email address format is local-part@domain-name, with the @ symbol introduced in 1971 on ARPANET

email-agent-chain-mua-msa-mta-mda.text: Email messages traverse a chain of agents: MUA (user client) → MSA (submission) → MTA (transfer/relay) → MDA (final delivery)

email-envelope-differs-from-header.text: An email's SMTP envelope addresses (used for routing) can differ from the header To/From fields — the envelope controls actual delivery, not the header

email-grounds-messaging-decomposition-in.text: Email independently demonstrates that messaging's two orthogonal decomposition axes (routing/content separation and temporal/spatial decoupling) are achievable in tandem with proven internet-scale reliability — its store-and-forward persistence with DNS routing achieves both reliability and clean decomposition, grounding the theoretical messaging decomposition in a concrete, decades-proven implementation.

justifications.label: Email proves that the theoretical messaging decomposition maps to a reliable, production-proven architecture

email-instantiates-both-messaging-decomp.text: Email is the single system that independently instantiates both orthogonal messaging decomposition axes — routing/content separation (SMTP envelope addresses for routing vs RFC 5322 headers for presentation) AND reliable temporal decoupling (store-and-forward with persistent retry up to 5 days) — making it the canonical complete messaging architecture from which both decompositions can be independently observed and studied.

justifications.label: Email contributes to both axes through independent mechanisms — protocol layering for routing/content separation and store-and-forward for temporal decoupling — and its instantiation of both confirms the axes are genuinely orthogonal rather than correlated

email-mandatory-headers-from-date.text: Per RFC 5322, only From: and Date: header fields are mandatory in an email message

email-mime-encodings-qp-vs-base64.text: MIME uses quoted-printable encoding for mostly 7-bit content with occasional non-ASCII, and base64 encoding for arbitrary binary data

email-mta-bounce-obligation.text: An MTA that accepts a message is obligated to either deliver it or send a bounce message back to the sender

email-mx-record-routing.text: The sending MTA queries DNS for the recipient domain's MX records to find the destination mail server for routing

email-original-reliable-temporal-decoupl.text: Email exemplifies reliable temporal decoupling at internet scale — its store-and-forward model with persistent retry (up to 5 days) and DNS MX-based routing discovery demonstrates persistence as the necessary precondition for reliable temporal decoupling, a pattern that predates modern message brokers by decades.

justifications.label: Email demonstrates persistence-for-reliability decades before Kafka formalized it

email-protocol-layering-separates-routin.text: Email implements clean protocol separation — SMTP envelope addresses control routing independently of RFC 5322 message headers which control presentation and identity, with the Received: header chain providing a reverse-chronological audit trail prepended by each relay hop.

justifications.label: Three base beliefs reveal email's layered architecture — envelope (routing), headers (content/identity), and trace headers (audit) each serve distinct protocol functions

email-received-header-prepended.text: Each SMTP server prepends (not appends) its Received: header field, so the trace reads last-hop-first

email-resilient-delivery-via-retry-and-r.text: Email achieves resilient delivery through DNS MX-based routing discovery combined with persistent retry queues (up to 5 days), built on the store-and-forward model that decouples sender and recipient availability.

justifications.label: MX routing provides path discovery, 5-day retry provides persistence, store-and-forward provides temporal decoupling

email-retry-window-five-days.text: Email messages can be queued and retried for up to 5 days before a permanent failure notification is sent

email-store-and-forward-model.text: Email operates on a store-and-forward model where neither sender nor recipient must be online simultaneously

email-store-forward-obligated-relay-chai.text: Email's store-and-forward model creates a chain of obligated relay agents (MUA → MSA → MTA → MDA) where each hop must either deliver or bounce, guaranteeing eventual resolution even when endpoints are offline.

justifications.label: The bounce obligation converts a best-effort relay chain into a guaranteed-resolution protocol — no message silently disappears

email-validates-reliability-and-decompos.text: Email demonstrates that reliable temporal decoupling and clean architectural decomposition are independently achievable properties — its store-and-forward persistence with DNS routing achieves reliability at internet scale while simultaneously maintaining clean separation along both messaging decomposition axes (routing/content and temporal/spatial), proving neither property requires sacrificing the other.

justifications.label: Email independently achieves both reliability and clean architectural decomposition at internet scale

eppstein-solves-loopy-k-shortest-paths.text: Eppstein's algorithm solves the loopy (non-simple) K shortest paths variant more efficiently at O(m + n log n + k), while Yen's handles the harder loopless constraint at higher cost

ergodicity-implies-stationarity-not-vice.text: Ergodicity implies stationarity but stationarity does not imply ergodicity

eu-digital-markets-act-2022-im-interop.text: The EU Digital Markets Act (2022) mandates interoperability for dominant messaging platforms; Meta opened WhatsApp/Messenger for interop in March 2024

eu-directive-2019-2161-personalized-pric.text: EU Directive 2019/2161 requires disclosure of personalized pricing via automated decision-making but permits non-personalized dynamic pricing based on market demand

events-broadcast-vs-messages-broader.text: In distributed systems, events are broadcast facts/state changes (loosely coupled), while messages are a broader category encompassing commands, events, and documents (targeted, explicit coordination).

events-vs-messages-distinction.text: In distributed systems, events represent state changes broadcast asynchronously for loose coupling, while messages are a broader concept encompassing commands, events, and documents used for explicit coordination.

eventual-consistency-anti-entropy-and-re.text: Ensuring replica convergence requires two mechanisms: anti-entropy (exchanging updates between servers to detect differences) and reconciliation (choosing final state when conflicts exist)

eventual-consistency-conflict-resolution.text: Last Writer Wins (LWW) is the most widespread conflict resolution strategy for eventual consistency, using timestamps or vector clocks to pick the latest write

eventual-consistency-liveness-not-safety.text: Eventual consistency guarantees liveness (all reads eventually return the same value) but not safety (intermediate reads may return any value before convergence)

eventual-consistency-three-repair-strate.text: Three repair strategies for eventual consistency: read repair (correction during reads, slows reads), write repair (correction during writes, slows writes), and asynchronous repair (background correction)

exactly-once-delivery-via-idempotency.text: True exactly-once message delivery in distributed systems is typically achieved through idempotency mechanisms at the application level, not through infrastructure-level delivery semantics.

exactly-once-via-idempotency.text: Exactly-once delivery in distributed systems is typically achieved through idempotency rather than true infrastructure-level exactly-once semantics.

exchange-two-market-states.text: Electronic exchanges operate in two market states: continuous trading (orders matched immediately on arrival) and auction/call (orders accumulated and matched at fixed intervals, e.g., market open/close)

exponential-backoff-core-formula.text: Exponential backoff uses delay t = b^c where b is the multiplicative base and c is the count of adverse events; binary exponential backoff (BEB) is the special case where b = 2

fan-out-high-coupling-indicator.text: High fan-out in software design indicates high coupling, negatively affecting maintainability and testability; fan-in (incoming dependencies) is the complementary metric

fan-out-messaging-fire-and-forget.text: In messaging, fan-out implies fire-and-forget semantics — the sender dispatches to one or many queues in parallel without halting or waiting for responses

fan-out-two-definitions.text: Fan-out has two distinct meanings: (1) a messaging pattern delivering messages to multiple destinations without blocking, and (2) a software design metric counting outgoing dependencies of a class or method

fib-heap-advantage-condition.text: Fibonacci heaps outperform binary/binomial heaps when decrease-key operations significantly outnumber delete-min operations (b << a): O(a + b log n) vs O((a+b) log n)

fib-heap-amortized-insert-o1.text: Fibonacci heap insert is amortized Θ(1)

fib-heap-decrease-key-o1.text: Fibonacci heap decrease-key is amortized Θ(1), achieved via cascading cuts that maintain the degree bound

fib-heap-degree-bound-log-phi-n.text: In a Fibonacci heap, every node has degree at most log_φ(n) where φ = (1+√5)/2; a subtree rooted at a degree-d node has size at least F(d+2) (Fibonacci number)

fib-heap-delete-min-o-log-n.text: Fibonacci heap delete-min is amortized O(log n) — the only non-constant amortized operation

fib-heap-dijkstra-improvement.text: Dijkstra's algorithm with a Fibonacci heap runs in O(|E| + |V| log |V|) instead of O((|E| + |V|) log |V|) with a binary heap

fib-heap-merge-o1.text: Fibonacci heap merge is amortized Θ(1) — concatenate root lists and update min pointer — strictly better than binomial heap's O(log n) merge

fib-heap-optimal-for-dijkstra-sparse-gra.text: Fibonacci heaps are well-suited for Dijkstra's algorithm when edge relaxations (decrease-key operations) significantly outnumber vertex extractions (delete-min operations), as the amortized O(1) decrease-key via cascading cuts enables the asymptotically fastest known bound of Θ(|E| + |V| log |V|) for arbitrary directed graphs with unbounded non-negative weights.

justifications.label: Dijkstra relaxes edges (decrease-key) far more often than it extracts minima on sparse graphs, exactly matching Fibonacci heap's strength

fib-heap-potential-function.text: Fibonacci heap amortized analysis uses potential function φ = t + 2m, where t = number of trees and m = number of marked nodes

fifo-narrows-spread-pro-rata-increases-d.text: FIFO matching narrows the bid-ask spread but encourages order flooding, while Pro-Rata produces greater quoted volume at the best price

file-locking-does-not-prevent-toctou.text: File locking does not prevent TOCTOU on filesystem namespace/metadata or on networked filesystems

file-sync-conflict-detection-version-vec.text: Distributed conflict detection in file synchronization uses version vectors; conflict detection requires maintaining a database of synchronized files

file-sync-e2e-encryption-recommended.text: End-to-end encryption (not just HTTPS or at-rest encryption) is the recommended mitigation for data privacy risks in cloud-based file synchronization

file-sync-offline-vs-shared-access.text: File synchronization supports offline operation and reconciles on reconnect via agent-based polling, whereas shared file access uses server-side push over always-on sockets

file-sync-star-topology-eliminates-spuri.text: Star topology (one hub, all others sync only with hub) is the recommended architecture for multi-location file sync because it eliminates spurious conflicts caused by separate per-pair archives

financial-exchange-instantiates-eda-comp.text: Financial order matching exemplifies EDA's complex event processing pattern — the order book functions as a thin-payload event structure (price, quantity, party) supporting continuous price discovery through the bid-ask spread, and the matching engine performs multi-event pattern matching (pairing compatible bids and asks based on price compatibility) that operates under stringent latency expectations.

justifications.label: Order books and matching engines map onto EDA's thin-payload and complex-event-processing design space under extreme latency constraints

financial-exchange-validates-eda-persist.text: Financial order matching validates EDA's persist-then-acknowledge reliability pattern at the extreme end of the frequency spectrum — the order book's continuous price discovery via complex event processing requires the same persisted-delivery guarantee that email and event brokers mandate, but at latencies where any data loss or duplicate processing directly impacts market integrity and price discovery, confirming the pattern's viability even under the most demanding temporal constraints.

justifications.label: Financial exchanges stress-test persist-then-acknowledge reliability at highest frequency

fir-filters-preferred-for-decimation-ski.text: FIR filters are preferred for decimation because only every Mth output needs to be computed (skipping intermediate outputs), whereas IIR filters require computing every output due to feedback dependencies

first-autocomplete-software-smartype.text: The first autocomplete software was Smartype (late 1980s), originally for medical transcriptionists in WordPerfect for MS-DOS

first-electronic-matching-chicago-1982.text: The Mid West Stock Exchange (now Chicago Stock Exchange) launched one of the first fully automated order matching systems ('MAX') in 1982

flash-storage-physically-append-only.text: Flash storage cells can only be written once before a page-level erase, making them physically append-only at the cell level; SMR drives are append-only per zone because writing to a track would clobber neighboring tracks

floyd-build-heap-linear-time.text: Floyd's bottom-up heapify builds a binary heap in O(n) time, not O(n log n), because most nodes are near the bottom where per-node cost is low

floyd-warshall-all-pairs-cubic-no-negati.text: Floyd-Warshall solves all-pairs shortest path in O(V³) time and works with negative weights but not negative cycles.

fok-ioc-aon-order-differences.text: Fill-or-kill (FOK) requires full immediate execution or cancellation; immediate-or-cancel (IOC) fills what is available immediately and cancels the rest; all-or-none (AON) requires full fill but can remain on the order book

fok-vs-ioc-vs-aon.text: Fill or Kill (FOK) requires full immediate fill or cancel; Immediate or Cancel (IOC) allows partial fill with remainder canceled; All or None (AON) requires full fill but can wait on the order book.

four-foundational-dhts-2001.text: The four foundational DHT systems proposed in 2001 are CAN, Chord, Pastry, and Tapestry.

four-major-gds-families.text: The four major Global Distribution System (GDS) families are Sabre, Amadeus, Travelport (Apollo+Galileo+Worldspan), and TravelSky.

fsm-dfa-exactly-one-transition-per-input.text: A deterministic finite automaton (DFA) has exactly one transition per state per input symbol; its transition function is δ: S × Σ → S

fsm-dfa-quintuple-definition.text: A DFA is formally defined as a quintuple (Σ, S, s₀, δ, F); a transducer as a sextuple (Σ, Γ, S, s₀, δ, ω)

fsm-equivalent-to-regular-languages.text: Finite-state machines accept exactly the regular languages — they sit at the bottom of the Chomsky hierarchy with less computational power than Turing machines

fsm-hopcroft-fastest-dfa-minimization.text: The Hopcroft algorithm is the fastest known DFA minimization algorithm

fsm-moore-vs-mealy-transducers.text: Moore transducer output depends on state only; Mealy transducer output depends on state + input; they are interconvertible (Mealy→Moore may add states)

fsm-nfa-to-dfa-powerset-construction.text: Any NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA via powerset construction, with potential exponential blowup in the number of states

gartner-apm-three-dimensions-2016.text: Gartner's 2016 APM model consolidated five dimensions into three: Digital Experience Monitoring (DEM), Application Discovery Tracing and Diagnostics (ADTD), and Application Analytics (AA)

gcra-is-atm-leaky-bucket.text: The Generic Cell Rate Algorithm (GCRA) is the ATM-specific version of the leaky bucket as a meter, standardized in ITU-T I.371 and ATM Forum UNI specification

gcs-continental-drift-10cm-per-year.text: Continental drift moves points up to 10 cm/year — significant for global datums like ITRF, negligible for regional ones

gcs-longitude-degree-varies-with-latitud.text: Latitude degree length is nearly constant (~111 km); longitude degree length varies from ~111 km at the equator to 0 km at the poles, proportional to cos(φ)

gcs-not-cartesian.text: A geographic coordinate system is not Cartesian — it uses angular measurements on a curved surface, not distances on a plane

gcs-requires-datum-for-completeness.text: A complete geographic coordinate system specification requires a geodetic datum — latitude and longitude alone are ambiguous without one; different datums yield different coordinates for the same physical point (differences can be hundreds of meters)

gcs-wgs84-gps-default-datum.text: WGS 84 (EPSG:4326) is the default global datum used by GPS with ~2m accuracy; ITRF provides subcentimeter accuracy accounting for continental drift

general-ledger-five-account-types.text: The general ledger contains five main account types: assets, liabilities, income, expenses, and equity

geohash-base32-z-order-curve.text: Geohash encodes lat/lon by interleaving longitude bits (even positions) and latitude bits (odd positions), then encoding every 5 bits as one base-32 character — a direct application of Z-order (Morton) space-filling curves

geohash-database-spatial-indexing-advant.text: Geohash enables efficient database spatial indexing (used in Elasticsearch, MongoDB, Redis, HBase) by providing contiguous range scans for rectangular areas on a single index

geohash-even-odd-cell-geometry.text: Even-digit geohashes produce square cells with equal lat/lng error; odd-digit geohashes produce rectangular cells with asymmetric error (longitude more precise)

geohash-linearizes-spatial-queries-with-.text: Geohash reduces multi-dimensional spatial queries to one-dimensional range scans on B-tree indexes by encoding coordinates as Z-order curve positions, but the prefix-based proximity guarantee is one-directional, creating false negatives at cell boundaries that require multi-prefix queries to compensate.

justifications.label: Geohash's Z-order linearization enables B-tree range scans but the one-directional prefix guarantee means boundary effects require multi-cell compensation

geohash-precision-by-length.text: Geohash precision scales with string length: 1 char ≈ ±2500 km, 5 chars ≈ ±2.4 km, 8 chars ≈ ±19 m

geohash-prefix-guarantee-one-directional.text: Geohash prefix guarantee is one-directional: a longer shared prefix means spatial proximity, but nearby points can have completely different prefixes (especially near the 180th meridian, equator, or prime meridian)

gifford-weighted-voting-1979.text: Gifford's weighted voting (1979) is the seminal work for quorum-based replica control, allowing each site/copy to be assigned different vote weights

git-commit-history-is-dag.text: Git commit history forms a DAG where vertices are revisions and edges connect parent-child commits; it is not a tree due to merges.

global-serializability-not-from-local.text: Global serializability across multiple databases can be violated even when every individual database independently guarantees local serializability.

go-back-n-receiver-window-1-discards-out.text: Go-Back-N ARQ uses receive window wr = 1, discarding all out-of-order packets and requiring retransmission of the entire window on loss; with N = 8 (3-bit), max wt = 7.

google-docs-storage-quota-june-2021.text: Google Docs files began counting against storage quota on June 1, 2021; files unmodified after that date remain exempt

google-drive-desktop-client-unified-2021.text: Google Drive for Desktop replaced both Backup and Sync and Drive File Stream in July 2021 as a unified desktop client with on-demand file access

google-drive-free-storage-15gb-shared.text: Google Drive provides 15 GB free storage shared across Google Drive, Gmail, and Google Photos — not 15 GB per service

google-drive-max-upload-size-5tb.text: Google Drive supports uploaded files up to 5 TB for non-native files, with a 750 GB daily upload limit

google-drive-search-uses-ocr-and-nlp.text: Google Drive search uses OCR for text in images/PDFs and natural language processing for intelligent file discovery

google-drive-sharing-three-permission-le.text: Google Drive has three sharing permission levels: can edit, can comment, and can view

google-proximity-operator-around-n.text: Google's explicit proximity operator is AROUND(n) where n is the maximum number of separating words; Bing's is near:n

gorilla-xor-compression-time-series.text: Facebook's Gorilla paper (2015) introduced XOR-based encoding for floating-point time series compression, which is widely used in TSDBs

gossip-aggregation-logarithmic-rounds.text: Gossip aggregation protocols compute network-wide aggregates using fixed-size pairwise exchanges and terminate in O(log N) rounds

gossip-protocol-eventual-consistency.text: Gossip protocols provide eventual consistency (convergently consistent), not strong consistency — new information reaches all nodes within time logarithmic in system size

gossip-protocol-no-reliable-comms-requir.text: Gossip protocols do not require reliable communication or a central coordinator; redundant gossip paths ensure information survives individual node or message failures

gossip-protocol-olog-n-dissemination.text: Gossip protocols disseminate information to all N nodes in O(log N) rounds due to exponential propagation where information roughly doubles in reach each round

gossip-protocol-two-families.text: Gossip protocols have two main families: dissemination protocols (spread data via rumor-mongering/flooding) and aggregation protocols (compute network-wide aggregates like max, min, sum, count)

gossip-provides-decentralized-ap-converg.text: Gossip protocols provide the decentralized convergence mechanism for AP systems — O(log N) dissemination without requiring reliable communication or a central coordinator achieves eventual consistency through redundant propagation paths, making gossip the natural convergence primitive for leaderless architectures.

justifications.label: Gossip's three properties (eventual consistency, logarithmic rounds, no reliable comms) converge on the same requirements AP systems face: convergence without coordination

gossip-seminal-paper-demers-1987.text: The seminal gossip protocol paper is Demers et al. (1987), 'Epidemic algorithms for replicated database maintenance'

gpon-oversubscription-up-to-256-to-1.text: G-PON oversubscription ratios can approach 256:1 due to point-to-multipoint architecture

graph-definition-vertices-and-edges.text: A graph is formally defined as G = (V, E) where V is a set of vertices and E is a set of edges connecting pairs of vertices

graph-strongly-vs-weakly-connected.text: A directed graph is strongly connected if every ordered pair of vertices has a directed path between them; weakly connected if connected only when edge directions are ignored

group-only-idempotent-is-identity.text: In any group, the identity element is the only idempotent element (proof: x·x = x implies x = e by left-multiplying by x⁻¹)

half-band-filter-m2-nearly-half-zero-coe.text: When decimation factor M=2, a half-band filter has approximately half of its coefficients equal to zero, reducing computation

happened-before-lamport-1978.text: The happened-before relation was formulated by Leslie Lamport in 1978 in the paper 'Time, Clocks and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System'

happened-before-strict-partial-order.text: The happened-before relation is a strict partial order: it is transitive (a→b and b→c implies a→c), irreflexive (a cannot happen before itself), and asymmetric (a→b implies b↛a)

hash-collision-pigeonhole-guarantee.text: A hash collision is guaranteed when the number of items exceeds the number of possible hash values, by the pigeonhole principle.

hash-cost-hierarchy-bitwise-mult-div.text: Computational cost hierarchy for hash functions: bitwise folding (fastest) > multiplicative > division-based (slowest); division is roughly 10x slower than multiplication on x86.

hash-division-method-prime-table-size.text: The division hash method h(K) = K mod M works best when M is a prime number near the table size, which helps distribute keys uniformly and reduce clustering.

hash-table-average-o1-worst-on.text: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time complexity for search, insert, and delete, but O(n) worst-case when all keys collide.

hash-table-dynamic-resize-maintains-amor.text: Hash tables maintain amortized O(1) operations through dynamic resizing governed by load factor thresholds — growing at α_max and shrinking at α_max/4 — absorbing the O(n) worst-case rehashing cost into the amortized bound while keeping the load factor within a constant band.

justifications.label: Load factor-triggered resize is what bridges O(1) average and O(n) worst case

hash-table-language-implementations.text: Hash tables underlie Python dict, Java HashMap, C++ unordered_map, and Go map.

hash-table-load-factor-formula.text: Hash table load factor α = n/m where n is the number of stored entries and m is the number of buckets; this is the primary factor governing hash table performance.

hash-table-o1-average-lookup.text: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case lookup via hashing, with collisions handled by chaining or open addressing

hash-table-resize-grow-shrink-thresholds.text: Hash tables typically grow when load factor reaches α_max and shrink when it drops below α_max/4.

hashing-evolution-culminates-in-distribu.text: The dynamic hashing amortization spectrum (hash tables → linear hashing → consistent hashing) explains why consistent hashing became the mandatory partitioning mechanism for distributed data layers: only the spectrum's endpoint achieves sublinear migration cost during topology changes, which is the minimum viability threshold for production distributed systems.

justifications.label: The partitioning requirement selects for sublinear migration cost, and the hashing evolution spectrum shows consistent hashing is the unique solution meeting that constraint

hashing-evolution-fully-validated-endpoi.text: The dynamic hashing amortization spectrum's convergence on consistent hashing as the mandatory distributed partitioning standard has been independently validated across theory (sublinear scaling), production (Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai), and historical origin (Karger's 1997 CDN use case) — confirming the evolution's endpoint was correct and not merely dominant-by-adoption.

justifications.label: The evolution chain (hash tables → linear hashing → consistent hashing → distributed partitioning) and the three-dimensional validation (theory/practice/history) independently converge on the same conclusion

haversine-better-than-cosine-small-dista.text: The haversine formula is preferred over the spherical law of cosines for nearby points because the cosine formula yields values near 1.0, causing floating-point precision loss.

haversine-earth-accuracy-limit.text: The haversine formula assumes a perfect sphere and cannot guarantee better than ~0.5% accuracy on Earth because Earth is an oblate spheroid with radius varying from ~6,357 km (poles) to ~6,378 km (equator).

haversine-formula-definition.text: The haversine formula computes great-circle distance between two points on a sphere: hav(θ) = hav(Δφ) + cos(φ₁)·cos(φ₂)·hav(Δλ), where hav(θ) = sin²(θ/2).

heap-optimization-spectrum-binary-binomi.text: Binary heaps, binomial heaps, and Fibonacci heaps represent a progression of improving asymptotic bounds on specific operations: binary heap has O(log n) decrease-key and O(n) merge; binomial heap improves merge to O(log n); Fibonacci heap achieves amortized O(1) decrease-key via cascading cuts and O(1) merge. The Fibonacci heap's asymptotic advantage materializes primarily when decrease-key operations significantly outnumber delete-min operations.

justifications.label: Each heap successor fixes the prior's bottleneck: binomial fixes merge, Fibonacci fixes decrease-key — forming a directed optimization path

hll-bias-correction-constant-formula.text: HyperLogLog's bias correction constant α_m is approximated as 0.7213/(1 + 1.079/m) for m ≥ 128

hll-memory-1.5kb-for-1e9-cardinality.text: HyperLogLog can estimate cardinalities exceeding 10^9 with approximately 2% standard error using only 1.5 kB of memory

hll-merge-is-elementwise-max.text: HyperLogLog sketches can be merged by taking the element-wise maximum of registers, enabling distributed/parallel distinct counting

hll-plus-plus-google-2013-improvements.text: HLL++ (Google, 2013) improves HyperLogLog with 64-bit hashes (eliminating large-range correction), empirical bias correction, and sparse-to-dense register representation

hll-plus-plus-targeted-pipeline-refineme.text: HLL++ is a targeted refinement of HyperLogLog's three-stage estimation pipeline rather than a fundamental redesign — 64-bit hashes eliminate the large-range correction stage entirely, and empirical bias correction replaces the analytical α_m formula at low cardinalities, validating the pipeline architecture's soundness while showing that two of its three stages had room for empirical improvement.

justifications.label: HLL++ improvements map to specific pipeline stages, confirming the three-stage architecture while refining individual stages

hll-published-flajolet-2007.text: HyperLogLog was published by Flajolet et al. in 2007, evolving from Flajolet-Martin (1984) through LogLog (2003)

hll-redis-commands-pfcount-pfadd-pfmerge.text: Redis implements HyperLogLog via the commands PFADD (add elements), PFCOUNT (estimate cardinality), and PFMERGE (merge sketches)

hll-refinement-confirms-analytic-optimiz.text: HLL++'s targeted refinement of HyperLogLog confirms that probabilistic data structures achieve efficiency improvement through analytically-guided pipeline refinement rather than fundamental redesign — each HLL++ improvement (64-bit hashes eliminating large-range correction, empirical bias correction for small cardinalities, sparse representation for low-fill registers) targets a mathematically identified weakness in the three-stage estimation pipeline, validating the analytic optimization approach established by Bloom filters' closed-form space model.

justifications.label: HLL++ improvements validate the analytic-refinement approach to probabilistic structure optimization

hll-refinement-valid-unless-conservative.text: HLL++'s targeted pipeline refinement confirms the analytic optimization approach for probabilistic structures, and this analytically-principled mergeability enables the distributed stream analytics toolkit — but the complete composition guarantee holds only when conservative update variants don't break the linear mergeability that distributed aggregation requires.

justifications.label: Analytic optimization and mergeable composition are jointly valid only when linearity is preserved

hll-relative-error-formula.text: HyperLogLog's relative error (standard error) is σ = 1.04/√m where m is the number of registers

hll-small-range-linear-counting-below-5-.text: HyperLogLog switches to Linear Counting (E* = m·ln(m/V)) for small cardinalities below 5/2·m when empty registers exist

hll-three-stage-estimation-pipeline.text: HyperLogLog achieves its 1.04/√m relative error through a three-stage estimation pipeline: harmonic mean combination of register values for outlier resistance, multiplicative bias correction via α_m ≈ 0.7213/(1 + 1.079/m) for systematic error compensation, and Linear Counting fallback below 5/2·m cardinality where empty registers indicate the harmonic mean estimate is unreliable.

justifications.label: Four base beliefs describe sequential stages of a single estimation pipeline — each stage corrects for a specific bias or variance regime, and the order matters

hll-uses-harmonic-mean-not-arithmetic.text: HyperLogLog combines register estimates using the harmonic mean (not arithmetic mean) to reduce the impact of outlier registers

hmac-integrity-authenticity-not-confiden.text: HMAC provides data integrity and authenticity but NOT confidentiality — it produces a tag but does not encrypt the message

hmac-key-longer-than-block-hashed.text: HMAC keys longer than the hash block size are first hashed to the hash output size; keys shorter are zero-padded to block size

hmac-md5-not-practically-broken.text: HMAC-MD5 is not practically broken for MAC purposes even though MD5 itself has collision vulnerabilities, but should not be used in new protocol designs per RFC 6151

hmac-prevents-length-extension-attacks.text: HMAC's nested two-pass construction provides immunity against length extension attacks, which is the primary motivation over simpler H(key || message) constructions

hmac-rfc-2104-fips-198.text: HMAC is standardized in RFC 2104 (1997) and FIPS PUB 198, designed by Bellare, Canetti, and Krawczyk

hmac-two-pass-hash-construction.text: HMAC is computed as H((K' XOR opad) || H((K' XOR ipad) || message)), a two-pass nested hash construction using inner pad 0x36 and outer pad 0x5c
