stable-storage-standard-disks-not-stable.text: Standard hard disk drives do not qualify as stable storage because they can return errors on read-back instead of either the prior or new data.

state-machine-replication-requires-deter.text: State machine replication treats replicated processes as deterministic finite automata and requires atomic broadcast and distributed consensus (often Paxos); used by Google Chubby and Keyspace

stop-and-wait-is-degenerate-sliding-wind.text: Stop-and-wait ARQ is the degenerate sliding window case with wt = 1, wr = 1, N = 2 (1-bit sequence number), equivalent to the alternating bit protocol.

stop-limit-order-non-execution-risk.text: A stop-limit order becomes a limit order (not a market order) when triggered, avoiding slippage but introducing non-execution risk if price moves past the limit

stop-order-becomes-market-order.text: A stop order becomes a market order when the stop price is reached and does not guarantee execution at the stop price; a stop-limit order becomes a limit order when triggered and may not fill at all if price gaps through the limit.

store-and-forward-convergent-messaging-p.text: Email and Kafka represent convergent evolution of the store-and-forward messaging pattern at different scales — email via obligated relay chains with bounce guarantees (MTA must deliver or notify), Kafka via durable append-only logs with consumer-managed offsets — both ensuring messages are never silently lost between producer and consumer.

justifications.label: Two independent systems converge on store-and-forward with different delivery guarantee mechanisms

strategy-pattern-behavioral-gof.text: The Strategy pattern (also called Policy pattern) is a behavioral design pattern from the Gang of Four catalog that enables selecting an algorithm at runtime via composition rather than inheritance.

strategy-pattern-open-closed-principle.text: The Strategy pattern supports the open-closed principle: new algorithms can be added without modifying the Context class.

strategy-pattern-three-participants.text: The Strategy pattern involves three roles: Context (uses a strategy), Strategy interface (defines the contract), and ConcreteStrategy (implements specific algorithms).

strategy-vs-state-pattern-distinction.text: Strategy and State patterns have similar structure but different intent: Strategy is chosen by the client/caller, while State transitions are managed internally by the object.

strategy-vs-template-method.text: Strategy uses composition (delegate to separate object) while Template Method uses inheritance (subclass overrides); both vary algorithm steps.

stream-analytics-complete-when-sketches-.text: Complete distributed stream analytics — exact state within partitions plus approximate aggregation across partitions — holds only when the underlying sketch update policy preserves algebraic mergeability, the property that enables combining partial results from different partitions without access to the original data.

justifications.label: Partition binding forces cross-partition analytics to rely on mergeable sketches; conservative update (a common Count-Min optimization) breaks linearity and thus mergeability — so the completeness of stream analytics is contingent on update policy choice

stream-analytics-requires-exact-state-an.text: Stream analytics platforms benefit from combining write-optimized exact state management (as provided by Kafka Streams via RocksDB's LSM tree pipeline for stateful processing) with mergeable probabilistic aggregation (HLL, Count-Min, Bloom for coordination-free distributed cardinality and frequency estimation), addressing complementary needs — local stateful processing that can exceed main memory, and distributed sketch-based analytics that merge without coordination.

justifications.label: Exact state (per-key updates, joins) and approximate aggregation (cardinality, frequency) are fundamentally different operations requiring different optimization strategies

stream-processing-90-percent-on-chip.text: Approximately 90% of stream processor work is done on-chip, requiring only about 1% of global data to be stored to external memory.

stream-processing-aos-vs-soa.text: Array-of-Structures (AoS) wastes cache on irrelevant attributes and breaks SIMD alignment; Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) enables contiguous data for efficient stream/SIMD access but risks cache misses when accessing multiple attributes of the same object.

stream-processing-complete-partition-and.text: Distributed stream processing at scale benefits from two complementary architectural concerns: reliable sequential persistence within partitions (supporting exact stateful processing through persist-then-acknowledge patterns) and probabilistic compensation across partitions (using mergeable sketches for approximate aggregation). Partition-bound exact state creates an architectural pressure toward approximate data structures as a natural complement for cross-partition analytics, though the evidence establishes these as independently motivated properties that address complementary concerns rather than proving them jointly necessary and sufficient in all architectures.

justifications.label: Partition-bound exact state forces probabilistic compensation for cross-partition analytics

stream-processing-generic-cpu-speedup.text: Stream processing on generic CPUs yields only ~1.5x speedup; dedicated stream processors achieve 10x+ due to efficient memory access and higher parallelism.

stream-processing-kernel-function.text: In stream processing, a kernel function is an operation applied to each element in a stream; kernels are typically pipelined and the same kernel is applied uniformly to all elements (uniform streaming).

stream-processing-memory-hierarchy.text: Stream processors use a three-tiered memory hierarchy: LRFs (local register files per ALU) → SRF (shared on-chip) → external memory, keeping temporaries away from slow external memory.

stream-processing-short-stream-effect.text: The short stream effect is a penalty incurred when stream datasets are too small to amortize kernel-switching and setup costs, making stream processing counterproductive.

stream-processing-srf-architecture.text: The Stream Register File (SRF) is a large on-chip cache shared across ALU clusters where stream data is staged before bulk transfer to external memory; compiler automates allocation.

stream-processing-suitability-defines-ac.text: Stream processing's three suitability characteristics — high compute intensity, data parallelism, and data locality — define the boundary between hardware-accelerable and general-purpose workloads: suitable workloads achieve ~10x speedup with ~90% on-chip execution, but workloads below the suitability threshold suffer the short-stream penalty (setup amortization failure) and degrade toward the ~1.5x generic CPU baseline, establishing a sharp rather than gradual performance cliff at the acceleration boundary.

justifications.label: Suitability criteria create a sharp performance cliff between 10x hardware acceleration and 1.5x CPU baseline

stream-processing-three-suitability-char.text: Three characteristics that make applications suitable for stream processing: high compute intensity (arithmetic ops to I/O ratio, ideally 50:1+), data parallelism, and data locality.

strict-avalanche-criterion.text: The strict avalanche criterion requires that flipping one input bit should flip each output bit with 50% probability.

strict-implies-cascadeless-implies-recov.text: In schedule recoverability: strict implies cascadeless (no dirty reads), which implies recoverable — each is sufficient but not necessary for the next

strictly-monotone-implies-injective.text: A strictly monotone function is always injective (one-to-one) and therefore invertible on its range

strong-eventual-consistency-crdts.text: Strong eventual consistency (SEC) adds a safety guarantee: any two nodes with the same set of updates will be in the same state, commonly achieved via CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types)

strong-eventual-consistency-resolves-ap-.text: Strong eventual consistency via CRDTs provides a complete resolution to the AP conflict problem — CRDTs guarantee that any two nodes with the same set of updates converge to the same state, eliminating the need for explicit conflict resolution that version vectors detect but cannot resolve.

justifications.label: CRDTs resolve what version vectors detect — but individually valid CRDT updates can still violate cross-field integrity constraints

strong-immutability-requires-final-class.text: Strong immutability requires all fields to be immutable AND the class cannot be subclassed (preventing subclasses from adding mutable state)

subsequence-clustering-sliding-windows-f.text: Subsequence time series clustering via sliding windows is fundamentally flawed — cluster centers converge to arbitrary sine patterns regardless of dataset

supercomputing-burst-buffer-is-distribut.text: In supercomputing environments, distributed caching manifests as a burst buffer.

svod-avod-tvod-revenue-model-types.text: VOD business models include SVOD (subscription fee, e.g. Netflix), AVOD/ASVOD (advertising-supported, e.g. Tubi), TVOD (per-title rental/purchase, e.g. iTunes), and PVOD (premium early-window pricing, e.g. Disney+ Premier Access)

swiss-cheese-model-non-aligned-holes.text: The Swiss cheese model of security states that each defensive layer has holes (weaknesses), but overlapping layers prevent threats from passing through because holes in different layers don't align

synchronous-replication-zero-data-loss-l.text: Synchronous replication guarantees zero data loss but adds latency bounded by speed of light (10 km roundtrip minimum ~67 μs)

task-scheduling-dependencies-np-hard.text: Optimal task scheduling with dependencies (forming a DAG) is NP-hard, requiring metaheuristic approaches

tcp-slow-start-exponential-then-linear-g.text: TCP slow start doubles the congestion window each RTT (exponential growth) until a threshold, then switches to linear growth (congestion avoidance, one packet per ACK).

tcp-window-16-bit-64kb-rfc1323-scaling.text: TCP uses a 16-bit window field (max 65,536 bytes); RFC 1323 introduces window scaling to support larger windows for high-bandwidth-delay-product paths.

temporal-decoupling-convergent-across-me.text: Temporal decoupling — producers and consumers need not be active simultaneously — is the convergent architectural property across all store-and-forward messaging systems, from email's obligated relay chains to message queues to Kafka's durable logs, each implementing the same decoupling at different scales and durability guarantees.

justifications.label: Store-and-forward convergence (email + Kafka) combined with message queue temporal decoupling reveals temporal decoupling as the universal property unifying all asynchronous messaging

temporal-decoupling-requires-persistence.text: Temporal decoupling in messaging is achievable without persistence (in-memory queues) but reliable temporal decoupling universally requires sequential persistence — the convergence of persist-then-acknowledge across all durable messaging systems (email, Kafka, EDA) proves persistence is the necessary precondition for reliable decoupling.

justifications.label: Temporal decoupling is the architectural goal; sequential persistence is its reliability precondition — all production store-and-forward systems combine both

tensor-sketch-outer-product-equals-convo.text: Tensor Sketch: the count sketch of an outer product x ⊗ x^T equals the convolution of two independent count sketches (Pham & Pagh 2013), enabling efficient polynomial kernel approximation.

thread-local-storage-provides-thread-saf.text: Thread-local storage provides thread safety by giving each thread its own private copy of variables that persist across subroutine boundaries

thread-safe-does-not-imply-reentrant.text: A mutex-based function is thread-safe but not reentrant — it will deadlock if called from a reentrant interrupt handler

thread-safe-means-no-race-conditions.text: A thread-safe function can be invoked simultaneously by multiple threads without causing race conditions or data corruption

thread-safety-three-levels.text: Thread safety has three levels: not thread safe, thread safe serialized (single mutex for all resources), and MT-safe (separate mutex per resource for finer granularity)

thread-safety-two-implementation-classes.text: Thread safety implementation approaches fall into two classes: avoiding shared state (re-entrancy, thread-local storage, immutable objects) and synchronization (mutexes, atomic operations)

three-conditions-for-conflicting-actions.text: Two database operations conflict if and only if: (1) they belong to different transactions, (2) at least one is a write, and (3) they access the same object

three-db-replication-techniques-tradeoff.text: Three database replication techniques: statement-based (problematic with non-deterministic functions), WAL shipping (tightly coupled to storage engine), logical/row-based (most flexible, decoupled from storage engine)

three-golden-rules-traditional-accountin.text: The three golden rules of traditional accounting are: real accounts — debit what comes in, credit what goes out; personal accounts — debit the receiver, credit the giver; nominal accounts — debit expenses and losses, credit incomes and gains.

three-pillar-immutability-dominance-requ.text: Immutability's singular ability to collapse the data and event planes into a single coherent architecture — the property that distinguishes it as the deepest pillar from logarithmic bounds (operational constraints) and CAP bifurcation (design choices) — depends on reliable causal ordering for conflict detection and convergence, which fails under Byzantine conditions.

justifications.label: Plane collapse relies on causal ordering (vector clocks) for convergence — Byzantine failures undermine the very mechanism that makes immutability architecturally dominant

three-resource-sharing-architectures.text: The three resource-sharing architecture types for distributed systems are shared-nothing, shared-memory, and shared-disk.

three-web-feed-formats-rss-atom-json-fee.text: The three major web feed formats are RSS (1999–2009), Atom (2005–2007, RFC 4287), and JSON Feed (2017–2020, using JSON instead of XML)

time-series-model-hierarchy.text: Classical linear time series models follow the hierarchy AR → MA → ARMA → ARIMA → ARFIMA, with V prefix for multivariate variants (e.g., VAR) and X suffix for exogenous inputs

time-series-stochastic-process-model.text: Time series data is generally modeled as a stochastic process where observations closer in time are more closely related than distant ones

timestamp-id-convergent-principle-diverg.text: Timestamp-prefixed ID design exhibits convergent evolution at the principle level (Snowflake and UUID v7 independently arrive at timestamp-in-most-significant-bits) while permitting divergent specialization at the implementation level (Twitter, Instagram, and Mastodon each optimize non-timestamp bits for their specific infrastructure constraints of datacenter topology, shard affinity, and decentralization respectively).

justifications.label: The timestamp prefix is a stable design attractor while remaining bits are free parameters for infrastructure-specific adaptation

timestamp-ids-complete-write-optimized-i.text: Timestamp-prefixed IDs (Snowflake, UUID v7) and LSM storage engines form a co-optimized ingestion pipeline — IDs generate monotonically increasing keys structurally optimal for append-oriented storage, which in turn maximally exploits the sorted structure design space, creating end-to-end sequential write optimization from ID generation through storage engine to disk.

justifications.label: ID generation and storage engine co-optimize for the sequential append pattern

timestamp-ids-ingestion-reliable-unless-.text: The complete write-optimized ingestion pipeline from timestamp-prefixed IDs through LSM storage achieves reliable monotonic ordering only when clock sources remain synchronized — clock skew or rollback breaks the monotonicity assumption that the entire pipeline from ID generation through B-tree locality to append-oriented flush depends on.

justifications.label: Clock synchronization is the single assumption underlying the full ID-to-storage optimization pipeline

timestamp-ids-optimal-for-append-oriente.text: Timestamp-prefixed IDs (Snowflake, UUID v7) produce monotonically increasing keys that align well with append-oriented storage engines — LSM trees benefit because sequential writes concentrate in the in-memory buffer and flush as sequential disk I/O, while B+ trees benefit from new records clustering at the end of the key sequence rather than scattering randomly across the index — creating a natural alignment between convergent timestamp-prefix ID design and write-path optimization in both storage structures.

justifications.label: The convergent ID design (timestamp prefix) and convergent storage design (append-only pipeline) are structurally co-adapted

timestamp-ids-reliable-sorted-indexing.text: Timestamp-prefixed IDs (Snowflake, UUID v7) provide reliably sorted, B-tree-optimized indexing where new records cluster monotonically at the rightmost leaf.

justifications.label: B-tree locality depends on monotonic ordering, which breaks under clock skew or rollback — the convergent timestamp-prefix design is only reliable with synchronized clocks

timestamp-prefix-id-convergent-design.text: Snowflake IDs and UUID v7 represent convergent evolution toward timestamp-prefixed sortable identifiers, arriving at the same design principle (most-significant-bits encode time) from opposite starting points (64-bit compact vs 128-bit standard).

justifications.label: Both embed timestamps in high-order bits for sort order and B-tree locality — the industry converged on this pattern across two independent lineages

toctou-canonical-example-access-open.text: The canonical TOCTOU example is access() followed by open() in a setuid program — an attacker replaces the file with a symlink between the two calls to escalate privileges

toctou-no-portable-fix-access-open.text: A 2004 impossibility result proved no portable, deterministic technique exists to prevent TOCTOU races using Unix access() and open() system calls

toctou-setuid-mitigation-seteuid.text: TOCTOU mitigation for setuid binaries: use seteuid(getuid()) to drop to real UID before calling open(), avoiding the need for a separate access() check

toctou-two-preconditions.text: TOCTOU vulnerability requires two preconditions: the check-then-use sequence is non-atomic (interleaving possible) and the checked property is outside the program's exclusive control

token-bucket-burst-size-formula.text: Token bucket maximum burst size is B_max = T_max × M, where T_max = b / (M − r), with M being the maximum link transmission rate

token-bucket-leaky-bucket-equivalence.text: Token bucket and leaky-bucket-as-a-meter are functionally equivalent (same conformance decisions given same parameters), but leaky-bucket-as-a-queue is not equivalent as it eliminates burstiness entirely

token-bucket-nonconformant-three-options.text: Non-conformant packets in a token bucket have three handling options: drop the packet, enqueue for later transmission (shaping), or mark-and-forward for possible downstream dropping

token-bucket-two-parameters.text: The token bucket algorithm is governed by two parameters: token rate r (controls average throughput) and bucket depth b (controls maximum burst size)

tombstone-gcgrace-too-low-risks-resurrec.text: Setting GCGraceSeconds too low risks data resurrection if a node is down longer than the grace period.

tombstone-is-a-write-causes-pressure.text: Tombstones are writes that occupy space and must be replicated, so mass deletes can create tombstone pressure that degrades read performance.

tombstone-prevents-deleted-data-resurrec.text: In eventually consistent distributed stores, a tombstone marker prevents deleted data from being resurrected by nodes that missed the delete operation.

tombstone-without-causes-reinsert.text: Without tombstones, a node unavailable during deletion would interpret the missing record on other nodes as a missed insert and re-propagate the deleted data.

topological-sort-linear-time.text: Topological sort runs in O(V+E) time, via either Kahn's algorithm (in-degree tracking) or DFS-based (reverse postorder).

traffic-shaping-vs-policing.text: Traffic shaping delays packets (adds latency, preserves packets) while traffic policing drops or deprioritizes non-conformant packets immediately (no delay, potential packet loss)

trailing-stop-only-moves-favorably.text: A trailing stop order adjusts the stop price automatically as the security price moves favorably, and the stop only moves in the favorable direction — it never retreats

trailing-stop-ratchets-one-direction.text: A trailing stop order adjusts its trigger price as the security price moves favorably but never moves the trigger in the unfavorable direction — it only ratchets.

transaction-commits-fully-or-rolls-back.text: A database transaction either commits fully or rolls back completely — partial commits are never allowed

transcoding-two-step-decode-then-encode.text: Transcoding is a two-step process: decode the source to an intermediate uncompressed format (e.g., PCM for audio, YUV for video), then encode into the target format

transrating-vs-transsizing-definitions.text: Transrating reduces bitrate without changing video format; transsizing changes the picture resolution (image scaling) during transcoding

tree-connected-acyclic-graph.text: A tree is equivalently a connected acyclic undirected graph or a graph where any two vertices are connected by exactly one path

trial-balance-partial-check.text: A trial balance where total debits equal total credits is only a partial check on recording accuracy — it does not catch errors of omission, errors of commission, or compensating errors.

trie-alphabet-reduction-trade-time-for-s.text: Alphabet reduction in tries (reinterpreting n bytes as 2n nibbles) cuts memory by 8x but doubles the number of node traversals.

trie-beats-bst-string-search.text: Trie string search is O(m) compared to balanced BST string search at O(m log n), because each of O(log n) BST comparisons costs O(m).

trie-no-hash-collisions-supports-orderin.text: Tries eliminate hash collisions, support lexicographic ordering, and guarantee O(m) lookup, but hash tables are better for secondary storage with high random access time.

trie-o-m-lookup-key-length.text: Tries provide O(m) lookup where m is the key length, and are optimized for string prefix operations such as autocomplete and spell-check

trie-operations-o-m-key-length.text: Trie search, insert, and delete operations are all O(m) where m is key length, independent of the number of keys stored.

trie-preorder-yields-lexicographic-sort.text: Pre-order traversal of a trie produces lexicographically sorted output, equivalent to a form of radix sort.

trie-radix-compression-spectrum.text: Tries and radix trees form a space-time compression spectrum — standard tries provide O(m) lookup with one node per character, radix trees compress single-child chains for space efficiency while maintaining O(k) operations, and DAFSAs achieve maximum compression by sharing suffixes across keys.

justifications.label: Three points on the trie compression spectrum trading node count for structural complexity

tsdb-data-uniform-append-heavy.text: Time series data is structurally uniform and append-heavy with fewer cross-table relationships than relational data, enabling specialized TSDB optimizations

tsdb-inherits-lsm-append-only-pipeline.text: Time series databases naturally inherit the LSM append-only write pipeline because TSDB workloads are structurally append-heavy — the convergence of append-only optimization in LSM (WAL → buffer → immutable flush) with TSDB's append-dominated access pattern explains why Prometheus and similar TSDBs use WAL + compaction architectures.

justifications.label: TSDBs' append-heavy workload is a natural fit for LSM's append-only write pipeline — Prometheus validates this with WAL + compaction

tsdb-originated-from-data-historians.text: Time series databases originated from industrial data historians used for sensor/SCADA data, and now serve DevOps, IoT, finance, and analytics

tsdb-three-key-optimizations.text: TSDBs provide three key optimizations over general-purpose databases: specialized compression, automatic data retention/downsampling, and time-oriented indexing

tsdb-time-as-primary-index.text: Time series databases use time as the primary index, which is the core architectural distinction from relational and NoSQL databases

two-phase-commit-cross-shard-atomicity.text: Two-phase commit (2PC) ensures cross-shard atomicity in distributed databases but creates performance bottlenecks; newer approaches like braided synchronization intertwine consensus phases across shards without global transaction ordering

two-phase-commit-distributed-atomicity.text: Two-Phase Commit (2PC) ensures distributed transaction atomicity: Phase 1 (prepare) asks all participants if ready, Phase 2 (commit) finalizes only if all agree

two-phase-commit-required-distributed-du.text: Two-phase commit (2PC) is required for durability in distributed databases — all participating nodes must coordinate before acknowledging a commit to ensure consistent state across nodes

two-phase-locking-vs-two-phase-commit.text: Two-Phase Commit (2PC) is for distributed transaction atomicity (prepare/commit across nodes), while Two-Phase Locking (2PL) is for isolation via lock acquisition/release — they are distinct protocols

uniform-revisit-outperforms-proportional.text: Uniform re-visit policy (visiting all pages at the same frequency) outperforms proportional policy (visiting frequently-changing pages more often) for maintaining freshness, because rapidly changing pages consume crawl budget but stay fresh for shorter periods

universal-hashing-collision-probability.text: Universal hashing guarantees a collision probability of 1/m for any two distinct keys, independent of input distribution, by randomly selecting from a family of hash functions.

unix-sysv-posix-message-queue-apis.text: UNIX provides two distinct message queue APIs: SYS V (older, using msgget/msgsnd/msgrcv/msgctl) and POSIX (POSIX.1-2001, documented in mq_overview(7))

uri-fragment-removal-changes-semantics.text: Removing fragment identifiers from URIs changes semantics because AJAX applications may rely on fragments for client-side routing, even though fragments are never sent to the server.

uri-ip-to-domain-safe-reverse-unsafe.text: Replacing an IP address with a domain name is valid URI normalization, but the reverse (domain to IP) is rarely safe due to virtual hosting.

uri-normalization-six-semantics-preservi.text: RFC 3986 defines six semantics-preserving URI normalizations: uppercase percent-encoding, lowercase scheme/host, decode unreserved characters, remove dot-segments, convert empty path to /, and remove default port.

uri-query-param-sorting-changes-semantic.text: Sorting query parameters alphabetically is a semantics-changing normalization because parameter order may be significant and the same key can appear multiple times.

uri-scheme-host-case-insensitive-path-se.text: In URIs, the scheme and host are case-insensitive, but the path is case-sensitive.

uri-unreserved-characters-set.text: URI unreserved characters (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, hyphen, period, underscore, tilde) should be decoded during normalization; reserved characters must remain encoded.

url-normalization-prevents-duplicate-cra.text: URL normalization (canonicalization) standardizes URLs via lowercase conversion, dot-segment removal, and trailing slash addition to avoid crawling the same resource multiple times

url-shortener-61-percent-failure-rate.text: A 2012 study found 61% of URL shortener services (614 of 1002) had shut down, most due to spam abuse.

url-shortener-base36-vs-base62-key-space.text: URL shortener keys can use base-36 (lowercase + digits, 36 chars) or base-62 (adding uppercase, 62 chars); base-62 yields shorter keys for the same address space.

url-shortener-brute-force-exposes-privat.text: The small key space of URL shorteners allows brute-force scanning that can expose private links, such as Google Maps directions revealing home addresses.

url-shortener-cctld-jurisdictional-risk.text: URL shorteners using country-code TLDs fall under that country's jurisdiction, creating legal risk (e.g., Libya shut down vb.ly for violating local pornography laws).

url-shortener-compactness-enables-brute-.text: URL shorteners' compact key spaces (base-36 or base-62 encodings) provide efficient HTTP-compliant persistent redirection via standard redirect semantics (301/302/307/308), but the same compactness that makes them useful simultaneously enables brute-force scanning that can expose private links — the desirable property and the vulnerability are structurally inseparable.

justifications.label: The compact key space that makes URL shorteners useful is the same property that makes them brute-forceable

url-shortener-post-body-dropped-on-redir.text: Browsers drop POST request bodies on HTTP redirects, which is a fundamental HTTP behavior that affects URL shortener architecture.

url-shortener-redirect-codes-301-302-307.text: URL shortening services use HTTP redirect codes 301, 302, 307, and 308, each with different semantics for caching, SEO, and whether the HTTP method is preserved.

url-shorteners-provide-persistent-link-i.text: URL shorteners provide technically sound persistent link infrastructure using well-defined HTTP redirect semantics (301/302/307/308) and compact key spaces (base-36 or base-62), capable of mapping billions of short URLs to long targets.

justifications.label: Technical design is sound but operational reality (61% service failure rate) undermines persistence guarantees

uuid-128-bit-32-hex-5-groups.text: A UUID is a 128-bit number represented as 32 hexadecimal digits in 5 hyphen-separated groups

uuid-rfc9562-current-standard-2024.text: RFC 9562 (May 2024) is the current UUID standard, obsoleting RFC 4122 and adding versions 6, 7, and 8

uuid-v1-v2-v6-expose-mac-address.text: UUID versions 1, 2, and 6 embed the host MAC address, creating a privacy risk that can be mitigated by using a random node ID with the multicast bit set

uuid-v4-122-random-bits.text: UUID version 4 uses 122 random bits (6 bits consumed by version and variant fields) and is the most widely used version for general purposes

uuid-v5-preferred-over-v3-sha1-vs-md5.text: UUID version 5 (SHA-1 hash) is preferred over version 3 (MD5 hash); both are deterministic given the same namespace UUID and name, and neither is reversible

uuid-v7-btree-locality-advantage.text: UUID v7 (and v6) provide B-tree index locality because new records cluster at the end of the key sequence, unlike v4 which scatters randomly across the index

uuid-v7-unix-ms-timestamp-sortable.text: UUID version 7 uses a 48-bit Unix epoch millisecond timestamp plus random/counter bits, producing lexically sortable and monotonically ascending identifiers without embedding MAC addresses

uuid-version-digit-third-group-position.text: The UUID version is indicated by the first hex digit of the third group (the high 4 bits of byte 7), and the variant is indicated by the most-significant bits of byte 9 (first hex digit of the fourth group)

vcs-centralized-vs-distributed.text: Centralized VCS uses a single server repository while distributed VCS (DVCS) treats every working copy as a full repository, with operations performed locally and synchronized via push/pull

vcs-history-sccs-rcs-cvs-svn-git.text: VCS historical evolution: SCCS (1972, first deliberate VCS) → RCS (1982) → CVS → Subversion → Git (distributed)

vcs-locking-vs-merging-concurrency.text: Version control systems use two concurrency models: file locking (exclusive write access, prevents conflicts but blocks parallel work) and version merging (allows parallel edits but requires conflict resolution)

vcs-revision-graph-is-dag.text: The revision graph in a version control system is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) due to merges, not a simple tree

vector-clock-biconditional-causality.text: Vector clocks provide a biconditional relationship with causality: x → y ⟺ VC(x) < VC(y), whereas Lamport timestamps only guarantee the forward direction (x → y ⟹ C(x) < C(y))

vector-clock-comparison-rule.text: VC(a) < VC(b) iff every component of VC(a) is ≤ the corresponding component of VC(b) and at least one component is strictly less; if neither VC(a) < VC(b) nor VC(b) < VC(a), the events are concurrent

vector-clock-detects-concurrency-lamport.text: Vector clocks can detect concurrent events (VC(a) ∥ VC(b)), whereas Lamport timestamps cannot distinguish 'a happened before b' from 'a and b are concurrent'

vector-clock-fails-under-byzantine.text: Vector clocks cannot reliably detect causality under Byzantine failures — this is a fundamental impossibility, not a fixable limitation

vector-clock-fidge-mattern-1988.text: Vector clocks are canonically attributed to Fidge and Mattern (1988, independently), though at least 6 earlier papers contained the concept dating to 1982

vector-clock-n-counters-per-event.text: A vector clock for n processes is an array of n logical clocks; each process maintains the full vector, requiring O(n) space per event or message

vector-clock-receive-increment-then-merg.text: On receiving a message, a process increments its own clock component and then takes the element-wise maximum with the received vector — both steps are required

vector-clock-three-operation-causal-prot.text: Vector clocks implement causal tracking using n counters per process with O(n) space per event: receive operations apply increment-then-merge (increment own component, then take element-wise maximum with the received vector), and causality is determined by strict component-wise comparison where VC(a) < VC(b) iff every component of VC(a) ≤ VC(b) with at least one strictly less, with incomparable vectors indicating concurrency.

justifications.label: These three mechanics constitute the complete operational specification of vector clocks — space allocation, update rule, and query rule — whose interaction produces the biconditional causality guarantee

vector-clocks-complete-causality-detecti.text: Vector clocks provide complete and reliable causal ordering for distributed systems, with biconditional causality guarantees.

justifications.label: Vector clocks' biconditional guarantee holds only in non-Byzantine environments; Byzantine failures fundamentally break the causal inference mechanism

vector-clocks-detect-concurrency.text: Vector clocks provide partial ordering and can detect concurrent events — two events are concurrent if neither vector dominates the other.

vector-clocks-detect-concurrent-events.text: Vector clocks extend Lamport timestamps to detect concurrent events, not just causal ordering — they can distinguish 'A before B' from 'A and B are concurrent'

vector-clocks-provide-strong-clock-consi.text: Vector clocks generalize Lamport timestamps and provide strong clock consistency (bidirectional): C(a) < C(b) if and only if a → b; Lamport clocks do not provide this

vector-clocks-solve-distributed-ordering.text: Vector clocks fully solve the distributed ordering problem — the absence of a global clock requires logical ordering, and vector clocks provide the strongest available solution with biconditional causality detection, strictly dominating Lamport timestamps.

justifications.label: Complete solution to distributed ordering — unless Byzantine failures invalidate the causality chain

vector-clocks-strictly-stronger-than-lam.text: Vector clocks provide strictly stronger causality guarantees than Lamport timestamps: biconditional (iff) causality detection plus the ability to identify concurrent events that Lamport timestamps conflate with causal ordering.

justifications.label: Lamport gives only one-directional implication; vector clocks give biconditional plus concurrency detection — a strict superset of ordering power

version-vector-sync-elementwise-max.text: When two replicas synchronize using version vectors, each element is set to the element-wise maximum (a merge, not a sum), and both vectors become identical after sync

version-vector-three-comparison-outcomes.text: Comparing two version vectors yields exactly three outcomes: ordered (one causally precedes the other), identical (same state), or concurrent (causally independent, requiring conflict resolution)

version-vector-vs-vector-clock-same-stat.text: Version vectors and vector clocks maintain identical state (a vector of counters, one per replica/process) but differ in update rules: version vectors track replica updates and synchronization, vector clocks track process events and message passing

version-vectors-complete-ap-conflict-lif.text: Version vectors provide the complete conflict lifecycle for AP systems — their three-outcome comparison (ordered/identical/concurrent) maps to the optimistic replication algorithm, while the AP convergence stack provides the infrastructure (gossip dissemination, Merkle anti-entropy) that delivers the updates for version vectors to compare.

justifications.label: Version vectors implement conflict detection within the AP convergence stack — anti-entropy delivers divergent state, version vectors classify it as ordered or concurrent

version-vectors-enable-optimistic-confli.text: Version vectors provide the causality mechanism for optimistic replication — their three-outcome comparison (ordered/identical/concurrent) maps directly to the optimistic replication lifecycle, where concurrent outcomes trigger conflict resolution and ordered outcomes confirm safe convergence.

justifications.label: Version vectors' three comparison outcomes directly implement the conflict detection step of optimistic replication's five-element algorithm

version-vectors-for-replica-ordering.text: Version vectors are specifically for ordering replicas by updates in optimistic replication systems, distinct from vector clocks which order events.

view-equivalence-three-conditions.text: Two schedules are view-equivalent if they agree on: (1) which transactions read initial values, (2) which transaction's write is read by each read (reads-from), and (3) which transaction performs the final write to each object

view-serializability-is-np-complete.text: Determining view-serializability is NP-complete, which is why practical database systems use conflict-serializability instead

vincenty-more-accurate-than-haversine.text: Vincenty's formulae provide more accurate Earth distance calculations than the haversine formula because they model Earth as an ellipsoid rather than a sphere.

vod-enabled-by-mpeg-compression-and-adsl.text: Two technologies that made video on demand possible were MPEG video compression (reducing raw ~200 Mbps TV signals to manageable bitrates) and ADSL data transmission (providing asymmetric bandwidth favoring downstream delivery)

vod-p2p-eliminates-linear-cost-scaling.text: Peer-to-peer VOD distribution eliminates the linear cost scaling of centralized streaming but introduces consistency, availability, and content control challenges

vod-server-placement-headend-cable-co-te.text: Cable VOD servers sit at the cable head-end (per market) while telco VOD servers sit at the central office or Video Head-End Office (VHO); placement determines latency and capacity

wait-die-younger-transaction-aborted.text: In the Wait-Die scheme, a younger (lower-priority) transaction waiting for an older transaction's lock is aborted, preventing circular wait

wait-for-graph-tracks-deadlock-cycles.text: A Wait-For Graph (WFG) is a detection structure that tracks cycles among waiting processes to identify deadlocks, used in both centralized and distributed systems

wal-bidirectional-recovery-enables-in-pl.text: WAL provides complete transaction infrastructure through bidirectional recovery — redo logs replay committed transactions for durability, undo logs roll back incomplete transactions for atomicity — and this dual capability enables in-place page updates rather than the more expensive shadow paging alternative.

justifications.label: Three base beliefs form a causal chain — redo/undo capability → atomicity+durability → in-place updates — where each enables the next

wal-bridges-acid-and-distributed-durabil.text: WAL is the universal durability bridge — it provides atomicity and durability for single-node ACID transactions, extends to distributed boundaries via 2PC's forced WAL writes, and supplies the append-only sequential foundation that LSM trees and Kafka inherit for write optimization.

justifications.label: WAL serves three roles from one mechanism: local ACID durability, distributed coordination (2PC), and write optimization foundation (LSM/Kafka)

wal-cap-agnostic-foundation-with-cap-spe.text: Distributed durability decomposes into two independent concerns with different CAP sensitivities — a universal durability foundation (WAL's append-only sequential log) that is structurally identical regardless of CAP positioning, and a CAP-determined compensation layer (gossip/anti-entropy/CRDTs for AP vs locks/consensus for CP) that varies based on the consistency-availability choice — revealing that the append-only primitive is the CAP-invariant bedrock beneath the CAP-varying compensation mechanisms.

justifications.label: WAL is the CAP-invariant durability bedrock; compensation is the CAP-varying superstructure

wal-checkpointing-bounds-recovery-time.text: WAL checkpointing periodically flushes all logged changes to the database and clears the log, bounding crash recovery time and reclaiming log space

wal-complete-backbone-unless-2pc-dual-fa.text: WAL provides the complete durability backbone from single-node crash recovery (ARIES) through distributed transactions (2PC) to storage engines (LSM write optimization), but the distributed leg is vulnerable — simultaneous coordinator-participant failure can leave distributed transactions in an indeterminate state that the WAL mechanism alone cannot resolve.

justifications.label: WAL covers single-node to distributed durability unless 2PC's dual-failure gap is hit

wal-complete-durability-backbone.text: WAL is the complete durability backbone for data systems — the same append-only sequential write primitive provides crash recovery (ARIES algorithm), distributed transaction atomicity (2PC extension to multiple nodes), and storage engine write optimization (LSM pipeline), making WAL the single foundational mechanism spanning single-node recovery through distributed persistence to write-optimized storage.

justifications.label: WAL unifies crash recovery, distributed durability, and storage write optimization under one mechanism

wal-complete-infrastructure-from-recover.text: WAL provides complete data infrastructure spanning micro to macro — at the single-node level, bidirectional recovery (redo for durability, undo for atomicity) enables in-place page updates that make practical buffer management possible; at the distributed level, the same append-only primitive scales through ARIES crash recovery, 2PC coordination, and streaming state management — demonstrating that one sequential write mechanism underpins the full architectural stack from individual page modifications to planetary-scale data systems.

justifications.label: WAL's micro-level bidirectional recovery and macro-level distributed backbone form complete infrastructure

wal-core-mechanism-for-system-level-dura.text: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) is the core mechanism for system-level durability: all changes are written to a sequential immutable log on non-volatile storage before acknowledging commit; recovery replays the log to redo committed and undo uncommitted transactions

wal-durability-backbone-completes-immuta.text: WAL's three-layer durability backbone (ACID → distributed → streaming) and immutability's scale-invariant validation (node → planet) together provide strong evidence that append-only sequential persistence serves as a primary durability mechanism across a wide range of granularities — WAL traces the functional extension from crash recovery through coordination to stateful processing, while immutability traces the scope extension from local to global, jointly covering much of the function × scale matrix.

justifications.label: WAL's functional axis (ACID → distributed → streaming) and immutability's spatial axis (node → planet) span the complete function × scale coverage matrix for append-only durability

wal-durability-backbone-extends-acid-thr.text: WAL's durability backbone extends through three successive architectural layers: single-node crash recovery (ARIES algorithm), distributed transaction coordination (2PC forced log writes), and stream processing state management (Kafka Streams via RocksDB's LSM pipeline inheriting WAL → buffer → flush) — the same append-only sequential write primitive serves as the durability foundation from ACID transactions through to real-time stateful processing.

justifications.label: WAL backbone (depth-3) covers ACID→distributed; Kafka/RocksDB inheritance (depth-2) extends it to streaming — the three-layer propagation from transactions through distribution to streaming is emergent

wal-durability-spans-full-cap-spectrum.text: WAL's three-layer durability backbone (ACID → distributed transactions → streaming) is CAP-agnostic — it underpins CP systems (via 2PC coordination requiring forced WAL writes), AP systems (via LSM append-only write pipelines in Dynamo/Cassandra), and streaming platforms (via Kafka/RocksDB state management) — making WAL the single mechanism that both CP and AP lineages inherit regardless of their consistency-availability choice.

justifications.label: The CAP bifurcation cascades into replication topology, concurrency control, and compensation strategy — but WAL is the pre-bifurcation primitive that both branches require, placing it architecturally upstream of the CAP choice

wal-enables-in-place-updates.text: WAL enables in-place database page updates, reducing index and block list modifications; the alternative approach is shadow paging which does not update in place

wal-guarantees-atomicity-and-durability.text: Write-ahead logging (WAL) provides atomicity and durability — two of the four ACID properties — not all four

wal-is-append-only-durability-mechanism.text: Write-ahead logging is the system-level instantiation of the append-only durability principle — WAL's defining invariant (sequential immutable log before page modification) is precisely the append-only storage property (immutable after write) applied to transaction durability, making WAL the bridge between the abstract append-only pattern and concrete ACID guarantees.

justifications.label: WAL's sequential immutable log is the append-only pattern applied specifically to transaction durability — the same structural property serves different purposes in LSM (write optimization) vs WAL (crash recovery)

wal-stores-redo-and-undo-information.text: A WAL log typically stores both redo information (to replay committed transactions) and undo information (to roll back incomplete transactions) for crash recovery

wal-transcendence-explained-by-append-on.text: WAL's CAP-agnostic universality (operating beneath the consistency-availability bifurcation) is a necessary consequence of immutability's status as the primary architectural pillar — since immutability has the deepest validation across all three structural forces and WAL is the canonical system-level instantiation of the append-only principle, WAL inherits immutability's structural priority over the CAP choice.

justifications.label: WAL's CAP-agnosticism follows from immutability's structural priority over the CAP bifurcation

wal-transcends-cap-through-immutability-.text: WAL's CAP-agnostic universality is explained by its alignment with the immutability universal — as the canonical system-level instantiation of append-only durability, WAL operates at a layer deeper than the CAP bifurcation, which is why it serves as the universal foundation beneath both CP and AP compensation strategies.

justifications.label: WAL transcends CAP because it instantiates immutability, which is structurally deeper than the consistency-availability choice

wal-write-before-modify-invariant.text: The defining WAL invariant is that all modifications must be recorded in the append-only log on stable storage before the corresponding database pages are modified on disk

wal-writes-log-before-database.text: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) writes prospective changes to a persistent log before modifying the database, enabling crash recovery to a consistent state

watermark-enables-incremental-sync.text: A watermark in data synchronization is a checkpoint that marks a position in time, enabling delta/incremental synchronization where only objects created, modified, or deleted after that point are transferred

watermark-provides-fault-tolerant-resuma.text: Watermark-based synchronization provides fault tolerance: if synchronization is interrupted, it resumes from the last watermark rather than restarting from scratch

web-crawler-deduplication-requires-proba.text: Web crawlers require two complementary deduplication layers — URL normalization provides deterministic exact-match deduplication (syntactically different URLs for the same resource), while SimHash provides probabilistic near-duplicate content detection (semantically similar pages with different URLs).

justifications.label: URL normalization catches syntactic duplicates, SimHash catches semantic duplicates — both are independently necessary for comprehensive deduplication

web-crawling-instantiates-universal-scal.text: Web crawling's dual deduplication stack (deterministic URL normalization for exact-match + probabilistic SimHash for near-duplicate detection) instantiates the same universal pattern seen in LSM trees (exact lookups + Bloom filter compensation) and stream analytics (exact partition state + mergeable sketches) — systems at scale universally supplement exact methods with probabilistic structures to extend coverage beyond what exact computation alone can afford.

justifications.label: Web crawling is another instance of the universal exact+probabilistic scale compensation pattern

web-push-uses-http2-single-session.text: The IETF Web Push protocol uses HTTP/2 and consolidates all real-time events into a single session for network efficiency

web-scale-infrastructure-requires-distri.text: Two primitives recur across independent web-scale domains: consistent hashing for data and request distribution (validated independently in distributed databases and distributed caches, suggesting domain-invariant convergence) and probabilistic structures for scale-compensated deduplication and approximation (instantiated by URL/content deduplication in crawlers, Bloom filters in LSM trees, and mergeable sketches in stream processing). The evidence suggests distribution determines where data lives and compensation extends reasoning beyond what exact computation alone can afford, though characterizing these as the only two orthogonal axes of web-scale infrastructure goes beyond what the current antecedents establish.

justifications.label: Consistent hashing and probabilistic structures solve the two orthogonal web-scale problems — distribution and approximation — independently validated across CDN, database, crawler, and analytics domains

websockets-full-duplex-sse-unidirectiona.text: WebSockets provide full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection; Server-Sent Events (SSE) are unidirectional server-to-client only

white-rabbit-sub-nanosecond-sync-etherne.text: Synchronous Ethernet combined with PTP (White Rabbit Project) achieves sub-nanosecond clock synchronization accuracy

work-stealing-communication-rule.text: In work stealing: under heavy network load, idle nodes should advertise availability; under light load, overloaded nodes should request help — this minimizes message volume

wound-wait-older-preempts-younger.text: In the Wound-Wait scheme, an older (higher-priority) transaction waiting for a younger transaction's lock causes the younger transaction to be aborted (wounded)

write-optimized-pipeline-instantiates-ap.text: The complete distributed write-optimized pipeline — timestamp ID generation through LSM storage to consistent hashing distribution — is the concrete end-to-end instantiation of the append-only principle's scale-bridging property, where the same immutability primitive simultaneously optimizes single-node sequential I/O and distributed coordination elimination within a single data path.

justifications.label: The abstract scale-bridging principle (depth-7) and the concrete write path (depth-7) describe the same thing at different levels — one identifies the pattern, the other instantiates it

xmpp-most-significant-open-im-protocol.text: XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol) is the most significant open IM protocol, XML-based, supporting server-side gateways to other protocols

xopen-xa-standard-scope.text: X/Open XA is the de facto standard for distributed transaction processing but explicitly does not cover long-lived distributed transactions.

yens-algorithm-edge-removal-guarantees-u.text: Yen's algorithm guarantees path uniqueness by temporarily removing edges from previously found paths that share the same root path up to the spur node, forcing Dijkstra to find genuinely different spur paths

yens-algorithm-finds-k-shortest-loopless.text: Yen's algorithm computes the K shortest loopless (simple) paths from a single source to a single sink in a graph with non-negative edge costs

yens-algorithm-k-shortest-loopless-paths.text: Yen's algorithm (1971) computes the K shortest loopless (simple) paths from a single source to a single sink in a directed graph with non-negative edge weights

yens-algorithm-published-1971.text: Yen's algorithm was published by Jin Y. Yen in 1971

yens-algorithm-root-spur-decomposition.text: Yen's algorithm generates candidate paths by decomposing each into a root path (shared prefix with a previously found path up to the spur node) and a spur path (new suffix computed via Dijkstra from the spur node to the sink)

yens-algorithm-space-complexity.text: Yen's algorithm has space complexity O(N² + KN) — N² for graph edges (worst case complete graph), KN for storing up to K paths of up to N nodes each

yens-algorithm-time-complexity.text: Yen's algorithm has time complexity O(KN(M + N log N)) when using Dijkstra with a Fibonacci heap, where K = number of paths, N = nodes, M = edges

yens-algorithm-uses-dijkstra-subroutine.text: Yen's algorithm uses Dijkstra's algorithm (or any shortest path algorithm) as a subroutine for computing spur paths from each spur node to the sink

yens-complete-k-shortest-paths-on-valid-.text: Yen's algorithm provides a complete, optimally performant solution for K shortest loopless paths on sparse graphs through the Fibonacci heap → Dijkstra → Yen subroutine chain, achieving O(KN(M + N log N)) time complexity.

justifications.label: Negative edge weights break Dijkstra's greedy invariant, invalidating the correctness of Yen's subroutine and the entire optimization chain

yens-fibonacci-sparse-graph-optimal.text: Yen's K-shortest-paths algorithm achieves its best asymptotic bound O(KN(M + N log N)) on sparse graphs by inheriting Fibonacci heap optimization from its Dijkstra subroutine.

justifications.label: Yen's K calls to Dijkstra each benefit from Fibonacci heap's O(1) decrease-key when relaxations dominate

yens-inherits-dijkstra-nonneg-constraint.text: Yen's K-shortest-paths algorithm inherits Dijkstra's non-negative edge weight requirement, making it inapplicable to graphs with negative edges without substituting Bellman-Ford as the subroutine.

justifications.label: Yen's correctness depends on Dijkstra's correctness; the non-negative weight invariant propagates through the dependency

yens-loopless-via-node-removal.text: Yen's algorithm guarantees loopless paths by removing all root path nodes (not just edges) except the spur node during spur path computation, preventing any path from revisiting a node

youtube-2-7b-mau-jan-2024.text: YouTube had 2.7 billion monthly active users as of January 2024

youtube-31-5b-ad-revenue-2023.text: YouTube generated $31.5 billion in advertising revenue in 2023, with combined ad and subscription revenue exceeding $50 billion from Q4 2023 to Q3 2024

youtube-500-hours-uploaded-per-minute.text: More than 500 hours of video content are uploaded to YouTube per minute

youtube-acquired-by-google-1-65b-nov-200.text: Google acquired YouTube in November 2006 for $1.65 billion in Google stock

youtube-coppa-fine-170m.text: YouTube was fined $170 million by the FTC for collecting minors' data in violation of COPPA; all 'made for kids' videos treated as COPPA-liable since January 2020

youtube-first-video-me-at-the-zoo.text: The first YouTube video was 'Me at the zoo', uploaded April 23, 2005 by co-founder Jawed Karim

youtube-founded-february-2005.text: YouTube was founded in February 2005 by former PayPal employees Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim

youtube-monetization-drives-server-side-.text: YouTube's shift to server-side ad injection (2024), protecting a significant portion of its $31.5B annual advertising revenue from client-side ad blockers, illustrates a pattern similar to CDN Edge Side Includes — both move content assembly from the client to the server/edge. This parallel suggests that when content integrity (whether for monetization or personalization) is a system requirement, server-side composition becomes an attractive architectural choice, and that economic pressures at large scale can independently drive adoption of server-side composition patterns originally developed for performance reasons.

justifications.label: Revenue protection at planetary scale independently converges on server-side content assembly

youtube-second-most-visited-website.text: YouTube is the second-most-visited website globally (after Google Search)

youtube-server-side-ad-injection-2024.text: YouTube introduced server-side ad injection in 2024 to circumvent ad blockers

youtube-tech-stack-languages.text: YouTube's tech stack includes Python (core/API), C (via CPython), C++, Java (via Guice), Go, and JavaScript (UI)

zero-heuristic-trivially-admissible.text: The heuristic h(n) = 0 is trivially admissible — it reduces A* to uniform-cost search (Dijkstra's), which is optimal but inefficient
